title: Column | Penn State's wrestling dominance might not be worth it
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s time to rattle the cage in Happy Valley, and Penn State is yet again flexing its athletic muscle and devotion to wrestling.

After a parade of athletic letdowns everywhere but the mat, the university is in a place where results haven’t cut it overall. I recognize some folks will try to spin the “why” of this differently, but there’s still one glaring potential solution staring the university in the face.

If you’ve read my last few columns, it’s pretty obvious. Penn State’s athletic department is a commercial juggernaut, with money pouring into every sport from hockey hype to basketball’s annual postseason disappearing act. Nobody’s even pretending to debate it.

I already crowned Cael Sanderson the most untouchable coach in Happy Valley and the sport’s kingpin. Hoarding national titles in a sport dripping with tradition is impressive, and the investment has built not only a college powerhouse but also a pipeline for America’s best.

But that also means it's comfortable leaving other programs to scrounge for what’s left.

The coach and administration’s approach to the sport is the reason the blue and white’s name carries weight, fills seats and lures top recruits.

This is Penn State, after all. What would it be without wrestling?

Fans and administrators love to point to the trophy case as proof that the plan works. That’s all well and good, but at some point it starts to look like overwatering a plant that’s already outgrown its pot. Pat Kraft bleeds wrestling, as evidenced by his always-occupied mat-side seat and his steady hand to keep the cash flowing. But it’s time to ask whether pouring more into a program already lapping the field still makes sense.

Frankly, it’s getting dull when the only suspense in Vegas’ books is who finishes second, while Penn State’s wrestling bench reads like a who’s who of talent. Instead of piling more gold onto a mountain, why not spread the wealth?

Start with a small tweak of resources and set clear benchmarks for other squads, and review the impact at season’s end; track the results and see if the grass gets greener. I’m not saying I could do better, but I think it’s worth trying for the benefit of other teams.

Shaking up the status quo won’t be easy. The wrestling faithful will dig in, and shifting budgets will mean managing change and bruised egos. Deciding which programs get a boost will ignite debate, but facing these impediments head-on builds trust and shows fans that winning is a goal, not limited to just the mat in Rec Hall.

That’s how you get more banners in rafters across campus, and grow Penn State’s fan base across all athletic corners.

Being a Nittany Lions fan means you’ve probably got a shrine of what happens on the mat. Lately, though, it all feels a little stale. Not the winning, of course. Other sports keep moving forward, while wrestling’s stuck in a cycle of dominance. The efforts to keep it are too immense.

Alums in the sport have given something to cheer about and have helped shape the wider wrestling community, but for a school branded as a wrestling powerhouse, the lack of current pro alumni is noticeable as the old guard begins to slow from competition.

It’s especially crucial today, as Olympic golds matter less to the public and youth. The current most notable former Nittany Lions wrestler is the UFC’s Bo Nickal, and while he embraces those college ties, he’s one of the sport’s most polarizing figures.

Sure, there have been all-time legends. But as the saying goes: What have you done for me lately? The Carter Starocci story is the only recent headline that has lasted, really, and it’s a negative one. It just doesn’t feel like the product has matched its previous level, though that can change with the flick of a light switch, especially with the current rostered talent. I’ll admit that.

Giving the mat a breather and shifting some attention to tennis, lacrosse or basketball can unlock potential and bring new energy to Penn State athletics. The tide always turns, and this dominance may not last.

Frankly, I can’t wait for there to be genuine competition again.

Our former wrestling reporter, Andrew Deal, called it months ago: Penn State isn’t even doing right by wrestling without a women’s program, even though adding one would be a major shift for the so-called home of the sport. And the demand is there, with women’s wrestling exploding at the high school level and the NCAA already designating it an emerging sport.

So, what’s the snag? Sure, the trophies keep piling up and Olympians keep rolling through, but is Penn State really pushing the sport forward? Winning is great, but developing the game matters, too.

The university needs to loosen the reins and let wrestling grow naturally, as it should, instead of funneling the sport’s development through a single room.

Winning and dominance could be achieved without its current approach, and it would mean more if the sport wasn’t just a pencil-in prediction that Penn State wrestlers bring home a national championship every year.

title: Film review | 4-star EDGE Elijah Guertin brings superior technique, blazing speed to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured its first true edge rusher commit in the Class of 2027 when 4-star Elijah Guertin announced his commitment on Sunday.

Guertin ranks as the No. 1 player out of Rhode Island and No. 27 edge in the class, per 247Sports composite. He helped Bishop Hendricken High School capture the Rhode Island Division I state championship as a junior despite a wrist injury that kept him out for most of the season.

He has also taken his athleticism to the Hawks’ track and field team, showcasing immense speed and mobility for his 6-foot-5, 255-pound frame. Guertin placed third in the 110-meter hurdles at the state championships last year.

Guertin’s film showcases his lightning-fast agility and bodily control. Combine that with a collegiate-level build and Guertin can make an immediate impact when he arrives on campus.

Here’s a closer look at his film.

Lethal run defense

Guertin’s agility and wingspan allow him to quickly shed opponents and reach inside to shut down interior gaps. Running backs will see open lanes and attempt to blast through, but Guertin uses cat-like reflexes to shut the door just in time, only needing one arm to stop the rusher in his tracks.

It starts on the line of scrimmage, as Guertin makes a heavy, targeted strike under his opponent’s arms to establish control. From that position, whether the running back goes inside or outside, Guertin pushes off his matchup and uses the momentum to kickstart his approach.

He possesses the mental quickness to immediately lock onto the ball carrier instead of trying to always collapse the pocket, and he plays a patient but fast-paced rushing game.

Once he gets a hand on the ball carrier, they’re both hitting the turf. Guertin puts his full frame into each tackle, whether he’s wrapping his arms around the opponent’s torso or he’s jumping on their back so they fall forward.

That said, Guertin doesn’t have to make every play on his own. He works in tandem with the rest of the defensive line by forcing the ball carrier into the middle where his teammates are waiting to pounce.

When a rusher does break through, he collaborates with the linebackers’ knee-high tackles by aiming his high to secure the down.

Quarterback hunter

When the quarterback drops back to pass, this is where Guertin’s speed and explosiveness shines brightest. He uses his ability to push away from opponents to break into the pocket, drawing the rest of the offensive line to collapse.

However, Guertin is often too swift and gets close enough to the quarterback to throw him off his game before the opposing line can stop him. And because he’s in the pocket so early, throws are often made before the receivers can get into position.

Using his height, he can get vertical to disrupt passes when the quarterback has enough time to avoid the sack. Sometimes he’ll cause the quarterback to scramble, forcing them to go through their progressions or settle for throwing the ball away.

Regardless, Guertin ensures that he always has a hand in the passing lane and times his attacks well to make deflections.

Guertin also doesn’t give up on the play if his initial burst is turned aside. Guertin displays the tenacity and adaptability required to free himself from his matchup and make a play on a quarterback who’s lingering in the pocket.

If the quarterback decides to take it himself, Guertin turns on the afterburners and hunts them down for a sack or forces an incompletion.

Body control

Guertin isn’t just a fundamentally sound player, he also uses his superior sense of balance and control to make the most out of any play. When he doesn’t have a clear lane through to the pocket, he uses a variety of different counter measures to give himself the advantage, including spin moves.

Guertin doesn’t wait for lanes to open, he actively takes initiative as a craftsman with great vision and urgency in attacking weaknesses. If he’s being pushed too far outside the pocket, a clean spin maneuver gets him right back in place and with the separation he desired.

Whether he’s attacking the inside or outside zones, he can tear upwards on the opponent's guards to force them off balance before he makes a break for the pocket. Couple that with nifty footwork, and it’s hard to keep up with him when he’s on his game.

Guertin doesn’t make unnecessary movements and always appears to have full control with what he wants to do. That allows him to change directions quickly, recover smoothly if needed and continue the fight from any angle.

When he’s on the ground, however, he’s not always out. There have been times where he’ll continue to be a pest by clutching tightly to the opponent's cleats from the turf, slowing them down just enough for his teammates to secure the tackle.

Each move is calculated yet fluid. Guertin has a set plan for each snap and thinks a step ahead in case his original route is shut down, but he isn’t afraid to make decisions on the fly.

title: Borough Council votes to advance comprehensive zoning ordinance draft following months of discussion
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
The State College Borough Council voted unanimously on Monday to advance the current drafts for a comprehensive zoning ordinance and accompanying Subdivision and Land Development Ordinance (SALDO) to be reviewed by the borough’s planning commission and its counterparts in the Centre Region and greater Centre County area.

The comprehensive zoning ordinance, which was discussed first by the council during their Sept. 8, 2025 meeting before being revised into the current draft circa the council’s June 1 meeting, is expected to modernize and provide better clarification to the borough’s existing zoning language and establish a balance between student and long-term housing.

The SALDO ordinance is expected to aid the comprehensive zoning ordinance by revising guidelines for how land is to be subdivided and developed under the new zoning regulations.

Among the proposed changes to the borough’s zoning include revised use cases for certain structures based on where they are zoned, revised regulations for future development in the borough and provisions to assist in the preservation of existing structures that were constructed prior to rezoning. A revised zoning map is also included in the zoning ordinance.

The borough is also planning on holding several neighborhood meetings in the coming months to discuss the zoning ordinance with specific communities.

Council President Evan Myers said it is important to have the vote allow the borough to advance something that is “taking forever to get done.”

“I've been working on this, either on council or on the planning commission, for 15 years — but I think it's longer than that,” Myers said. “It's okay to do things right and take time to decide, but at some point, this council is charged with the responsibility to make a decision.”

Myers pointed to the housing crisis, both locally and nationally, and Penn State’s plans to increase enrollment at University Park. He said some of the issues can be better addressed with updated and improved zoning guidelines.

“Will we have to revise this at some point? I suspect so, but it's time,” Myers said. “Yes, we're going to go through all these different things and ask these questions and see what we can work in but, ultimately, this council will decide, and there may be some four-to-three votes that we take, and that's how things work.”

Rachel Brimmer, a State College resident since 2009, said she only knows one other colleague out of the 18 full-time employees of Penn State’s Agricultural Analytical Services Laboratory who also lives in State College. The 16 others have to commute from Penns Valley, Phillipsburg or Altoona.

“All of us have bachelor's degrees or more, and most of us cannot live in the State College area,” Brimmer, who works as an Agricultural and Environmental Lab Technologist, said.

Brimmer said she saw a need for the borough to increase the amount of land-trust homes, and thus affordable housing, within the borough for people to have better access to on-campus activities. Brimmer has been a land-trust homeowner for over two years.

Brimmer said the borough could use the comprehensive zoning ordinance to assist in this while improving regulations for existing structures, with Brimmer proposing the allowance of constructing other structures on land-trust properties.

“I occasionally go to School of Music recitals and the clubs that bring renowned musicians, and there might be 30 to 50 people in that School of Music Recital Hall,” Brimmer said. “That's a massive amount of free engagement opportunities that are just not available to people who are commuting 30, 45 minutes, back and forth, every day.”

Cynthia Carpenter, another State College resident, said while she approves the zoning ordinance being adopted, she believes the borough should also tackle existing housing issues, such as an abundance of short-term rental homes in the borough.

“People don't like so many empty houses, which they view as a breakdown in the neighborhood, whether they are short-term rental or an investment property for football houses,” Carpenter said. “The demand for these houses has raised the prices and made them less affordable for individual homeownership by people who live and work here. People like having families, children and neighbors that they can build community with.”

Carpenter said the zoning ordinance’s provision for triplex and quadplex residential structures were another point of concern, and their construction would not work to significantly ease housing affordability issues.

“These units will be investment rental properties. It's going to be people from outside our area building and, if it's low-income or affordable housing, the property probably will not be kept up,” Carpenter said. “It's going to remove modest, affordable, owner-occupied homes. We have quite a large stock of modest homes. They should be affordable.”

title: Film review | 3-star Caleb Cooper brings full package at safety
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State earned a commitment from 3-star safety Caleb Cooper last Monday, giving the Nittany Lion a second safety to commit in the 2027 class.

Cooper committed just a day after receiving an offer and finishing his visit in Happy Valley.

The 6-foot safety ranks as the No. 856 player, No. 62 athlete and No. 31 player in North Carolina, according to 247Sports composite, adding to the nationwide approach to Penn State’s 2027 class.

Cooper brings a strong skillset as a true safety, getting the freedom to roam the field while playing up in the box to stop the run, as well.

Here’s a look at what stands out in Cooper's film.

Playmaker as deep safety

As a true safety, Cooper doesn’t have much tape in man-to-man. However, he makes up for that with his zone skills and is the last line of defense on his team. Many times, Cooper has the freedom to read the play and move to either side of the field mid-play to add help on a receiver.

Cooper shows his playmaking ability, something Penn State has thrived on at the safety position over the past five years, reading the play and utilizing his skillset to change a game.

He could add to an illustrious group of names, including one of the top turnover makers, Zakee Wheatley, to make an impact at safety and make it to the next level.

Reading quarterbacks’ eyes

While Cooper showed he excels at reading a play from well beyond the line of scrimmage, he really proved his strength in reading the quarterback’s eyes. Whether it’s intercepting a ball, breaking up a pass or making a big hit to halt the play, his eyes remain on the quarterback to see where the football is going.

Reading the quarterback’s eyes is something that he’ll need to continue to develop at the next level, as the skill level of the position takes a massive jump. At the collegiate level is where quarterbacks will start to deceive safeties like Cooper, but if he continues to grow he’ll adapt to the next level.

He shows a natural ability to be where the ball is on most plays, giving him the opportunity to be a playmaker.

Cooper’s ability to read the quarterback’s eyes at a high rate already allows him to continue to grow as an all-around safety, and something Penn State’s coaching staff can tweak to develop him into a potential starter.

Run stopper

Some safeties’ strengths are just in reading a play and being 20 years from the line of scrimmage, others are stronger playing in the box, almost acting as an additional linebacker. Cooper does both despite standing at 6-foot, 207 pounds.

His ability to not only read passes, but read run plays makes him an all-around safety and something that Penn State can utilize around the field. This style matches that of current safety Marcus Neal Jr. who saw a lot of time in the box despite playing safety.

Cooper’s ability to make a difference in the box will be helpful at the collegiate level, as he’s proved to be a heavy hitter when he meets the ball carrier. This allows the coaching staff to move him around the field to allow him to be a full playmaker.

The safety’s all-around play makes him an intriguing prospect and could be another diamond in the rough for Matt Campbell as he attempts to develop talent in the Big Ten.

title: Recruiting roundup | 3 commits, 3 flipped players highlight Penn State recruiting news
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
It was an interesting week for Penn State’s recruiting efforts in the Class of 2027.

While the week started off strong with a pair of commitments, the energy deflated in the coming days with a pair of cornerback pledges electing to flip.

On Wednesday, the second summer prospect camp was held for 2028 recruits and featured some of the top quarterbacks. Matt Campbell and company then hosted five players for official visits on Friday, ending the week by securing a blue-chip defensive end commitment. While the week ended on a high note, the day after was grim as a 4-star wide receiver flipped.

Here’s a recap of Penn State’s recent up-and-down week in the recruiting department.

Commitments

Penn State appeared steady heading into the week after hosting a large group of both committed and uncommitted recruits during its first official visit weekend on June 5.

The Nittany Lions then went on a roll, securing two commitments on Monday and then a blue-chip defensive end on Sunday.

Each player filled a specific positional role that hadn’t been met yet. Long snapper Clayton Powell became the first special teams commitment in the class, safety Caleb Cooper became just the second safety on board and defensive end Elijah Guertin is Christian Smith’s first defensive end.

Additionally, Campbell expanded his geographical range to states that he hadn’t previously seen success from. Cooper hails from North Carolina, Guertin from Rhode Island and Powell from Louisiana, showcasing how this new staff continues to broaden their recruiting horizons.

Even better for Campbell, Guertin is the No. 1 player out of Rhode Island, so he’s been finding success in New England with Guertin and quarterback Will Wood while continuing to attack the northeast pipeline for the Class of 2028.

Like Wood in Massachusetts, Guertin is a private high school elite, having recently won the 2025 Rhode Island Division I state championship with Bishop Hendricken. At one point, he returned from a wrist injury wearing a cast before putting up 20 tackles and two sacks in five games.

Guertin’s commitment came quickly after his official visit to Happy Valley on Friday, but Campbell will still have to fend off Tennessee, which has an official visit with Guertin planned for this Friday.

Players flipped

Penn State lost a pair of 3-star cornerbacks and a 4-star wide receiver that flipped to schools outside of the Big Ten.

Semajay Robinson was Matt Campbell’s first commit in the 2027 class. He was brought in by Terry Smith on March 28, and it appeared as though he was fully locked in with the Nittany Lions after his official visit on June 5.

Robinson had also been extremely active on social media platforms such as X and Instagram, making an effort to draw in more recruits to Happy Valley.

That was until Robinson removed all traces of Penn State from his social media accounts on Tuesday morning. Later that day, he announced that he’d flipped to Virginia, becoming the Cavaliers’ highest-ranked commit in the class.

Zachary Gleason Jr. followed the same trend two days later, but he headed next door to West Virginia to become the Mountaineers' only cornerback commit.

He had also been recruited by Smith and originally committed to Penn State on March 31, just three days after Robinson. With Robinson and Gleason Jr. out the door, Smith has two cornerbacks remaining in the class.

Jamir Dean was the third flip in seven days. He committed to Penn State on May 1, but that only caught Georgia’s attention, as the Bulldogs made Dean an offer five days later and continued to push for him despite his social media presence repping the blue and white.

Dean made his way out to Athens for an official visit on May 29 before heading to State College for the June 5 visit, a weekend that seemingly went well. However, Dean couldn’t resist Georgia and flipped on Monday.

Now, the Nittany Lions have only one receiver commit in 4-star Landon Blum, but have their sights set on top prospects Khalil Taylor and DeShawn Hall to replenish Kashif Moore’s ranks.

Uncommitted visitors

Three of the five visitors on Friday have yet to commit, and all three are currently rated 3-star recruits.

Dhillon McGee may be able to replenish Smith’s cornerback room. He’s the No. 67 player out of Texas and No. 48 cornerback in the Class of 2027, and Penn State is the only northern school still in the mix.

McGee’s being hunted by in-state schools Texas and SMU, and he has an official visit to the Longhorns planned for Friday.

Offensive tackles Lucas Rhoa and Oscar Webersink were also in town this weekend. Rhoa is the No. 47 player out of California and the No. 54 offensive tackle, while Webersink is the No. 3 player from Connecticut and No. 75 offensive tackle.

Rhoa is notably joining McGee at Texas on Friday, and Webersink has Florida State and Maryland on his trail despite no official visits set up with them.

Rhoa and Webersink — if both choose Penn State — add depth and potential for competition in the room.

Class of 2028 quarterback news

Penn State hosted a prospect camp starting on Wednesday, where a few notable quarterbacks were in attendance.

Headlining the camp was 4-star quarterback James Armstrong. The in-state prospect is the No. 4 player out of Pennsylvania and No. 10 quarterback in the 2028 class, receiving an offer from Penn State in late March.

Armstrong made an unofficial visit out to State College last September as well, the only one recorded per 247Sports.

Quarterbacks coach Jake Waters also worked closely with fellow 4-star quarterback Trey Tagliaferri, who’s the No. 9 player out of New Jersey and No. 14 quarterback in the class.

title: A summer guide to spending a weekend in State College
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Every summer, State College feels like a completely different town than when school is in session. With the majority of Penn State students returning home, traveling or away at internships, the town feels empty, but townies feel at home.

Here’s a list of recommendations for spending a summer weekend in State College.

Local classics

The State Theatre

The State Theatre has been a go-to spot since its founding in 1938. Located across from Old Main on West College Avenue, the theater offers live music and movie showings, varying weekly.

The Arboretum

The Arboretum at Penn State is a welcoming place for visitors and locals alike. The Arboretum features the H.O. Smith Botanic Gardens, the Childhood’s Gate Children’s Garden, a pollinator and bird garden, a trail system and more.

The Berkey Creamery and Meyer Dairy Farms

Being two of State College’s iconic ice cream spots, they’re often the center of intense debate over which one is better.

The Berkey Creamery, known simply as “the Creamery,” is run by Penn State and located on campus. It is known for generous scoops and iconic flavors, as well as training the founders of Ben and Jerry’s Ice Cream.

Meyer Dairy Farms is a 10 minute drive from campus and creates all their dairy products on site. It sells fresh milk, ice cream and other locally produced products.

Both stores are mainstays in the community — the Creamery was founded in 1865 and Meyer Dairy Farms in 1887 — and either one can keep you cool on a hot summer day.

East End Social

East End Social is a new initiative in downtown State College to bring the community together. From May to August, the 100 block of Hiester Street will be closed to cars to host block parties and other social events as part of it.

An event calendar can be found here.

Breakfast spots

Sowers Harvest Cafe

Located in downtown State College, Sowers uses local and ethically sourced ingredients. The handmade soft pretzel is a Central Pennsylvania staple.

Rothrock Coffee

This coffee shop produces high-quality coffee, roasted in small batches on site. The cafe, located on South Atherton Street, also serves breakfast and lunch items.

The Original Waffle Shop

There are two Original Waffle Shop locations, located north and west of campus. The restaurant is known for its expansive breakfast and brunch menu.

Lunch and dinner spots

The Corner Room

Located on the corner of East College Avenue and South Allen Street, The Corner Room is one of downtown State College’s most well-known restaurants. The restaurant offers breakfast, lunch, dinner, drinks and desserts, including the classic grilled stickies.

Tadashi Japanese Restaurant

This restaurant has two locations downtown, each offering a different style of cuisine. The smaller of the two, located on College Avenue, serves mostly ramen, while the newer, larger restaurant on South Atherton serves sushi and udon.

India Pavilion

India Pavilion has been a part of the State College community since 1997, serving North Indian-style food. It offers an all-you-can-eat lunch buffet every day except Tuesday for $15.95 per person.

Gigi’s Southern Table

Open for brunch, lunch and dinner, Gigi’s offers southern-style food like fried green tomatoes and creole chicken. The ingredients are sourced locally for a farm-to-table experience.

Pine Grove Hall

This is the perfect choice for those looking to combine dinner and drinks with local music. Located in Pine Grove Mills, the food is sourced from local farmers and restaurants, and the performers are often local musicians.

Drinks

Doggie’s Pub

Doggie’s, located downtown, is a popular spot to take in the summer atmosphere. The pub offers live music, monthly events and a dog-friendly beer garden.

Bill Pickle’s Tap Room

Pickle’s is known for its Tuesday Country Nights and its barbecue. It is located centrally on South Allen Street.

Creative excursions

The Palmer Museum of Art

Palmer reopened last summer following renovations and a location change. It’s located adjacent to the Penn State Arboretum, combining artwork with nature.

The Makery

This creative space is located downtown in the Martin Luther King Jr. Plaza. The space offers art lessons and open studio space, as well as the Makery Market, which features artwork from Central Pennsylvania artists.

Webster’s Bookstore and Cafe

Webster’s is a spot for local music, vintage shopping, book shopping, coffee and breakfast food. It often hosts events, including drag shows and book launches and has an online book store.

2000 Degrees

This paint-your-own-pottery studio in downtown State College welcomes walk-ins. However, reservations are required for groups larger than five.

The Squirrel and Acorn Bookshop

A newer addition to downtown State College, opening in 2022, this bookshop sells stories from independent booksellers along with stationary and other book-related goods.

Outdoor activities

Shaver’s Creek Environmental Center

Shaver’s Creek is a 25 minute drive from downtown State College and a place to explore local nature. It’s celebrating its 50th anniversary this year and features hiking trails, the Klingsberg Aviary and the Litzinger Discovery Room.

Jo Hays Vista

Located along PA 26 S, Jo Hays Vista offers accessible views of State College. It’s located near the trail heads of the Mid State Trail and Jackson Trail, providing easy access to local trails.

WingFest at Tussey Mountain

Offered every Thursday until August 6, WingFest is a way to experience local music and cuisine. Attendees are able to taste local wings and vote in the annual competition.

Where to stay

The Nittany Lion Inn

The Nittany Lion Inn is close to both campus and downtown. It offers a number of dining choices, including Triplett’s for American food, Lionne & 1855 Lounge for French and American cuisine and Dear Joe for coffee and breakfast.

The Toftrees Golf Resort

For those looking to combine their visit with local golfing, Toftrees Gold Resort is the perfect place to stay. It also has The Field Burger and Tap, a restaurant where diners can try the Matt Campbell burger.

State College doesn’t have extensive public transportation, so a car is recommended for those looking to travel outside of downtown and campus. CATA bus offers services around State College, but its routes are reduced during the summer.

title: Column | A reality check
author: Teresa Phelan | Opinions Editor
description:
Maybe it was arrogance or some sort of delusion, but I thought I was immune to all the bad things that come with being a freshman on a college campus. Immune to homesickness, immune to late nights wondering if this was the right choice.

Surprise, I wasn’t. I was a mess, and quite honestly, a loser. I think the version of myself that couldn’t even imagine graduating because I was so miserable would be in awe of the person who’s writing this column.

Coming from a Penn State family meant only one thing: After graduating from high school, I would make my way to State College in the fall.

Upon my arrival, I quickly realized two things. One, I wasn’t happy. Two, everyone else was.

The perfect, ideal college aesthetic was a figment of my imagination, and every day just meant that I was one day closer to being able to pack up all my things and go home.

I think about everything I missed out on because I was some shell of a human that couldn’t realize what was in front of her.

I understand how this is coming off, though. I should’ve stopped moping around and actually made some change for myself instead of wallowing in my misery, but hindsight is 20/20.

It’s easy to say that I should’ve done something different, stopped complaining and actually gotten my things together. I was in a constant limbo of wanting to go anywhere but here, but recognizing that nothing was going to change unless I did.

So, that’s what I did. And what every first-year student needs to do: Put on big girl pants and take control of the things you want.

If you want to change your major, do it. If your friends want to get Mexican food at 11 p.m., go.

I can’t stress the importance of spontaneity enough. Don’t be stupid, but you’ll thank yourself.

As I’m writing this, I’m contemplating whether the major I convinced myself was perfect for me truly is. I could spiral and spend all night mad at myself, because maybe I should’ve spent more time thinking it through, but it’s too late. And to 18-year-old-me, it was right.

Sometimes you’ll make decisions that conflict with something you once believed in, and it will make your head and stomach ache, but you need to suppress it. Not in an “ignore your mental health” way, but understanding that growing up means being uncomfortable with new situations, people and things that push you to be a different version of yourself.

A version of yourself that can take a punch to the ego, a version of yourself that can stand up and ask for help.

There are a lot of hard truths you have to face head-on. You might not like your roommate and you might not get the internship you definitely thought you had in the bag, but you need to get over it.

Dwelling in your sorrow and misery isn’t going to fix the situation at hand and once it finally clicks that it’s out of your control, it all changes.

So much of your time can, and probably will, be wasted thinking about what could have been said or done differently, but what’s that accomplishing… nothing. Focusing on things in your control and within your means to change is what’s going to make you happy and contribute to a successful time at Penn State.

Not everything is sunshine and rainbows all the time, and don’t let anyone convince you otherwise. No one is thriving 100% of the time, and although their Instagram feeds say differently, it’s not their reality and it certainly doesn’t have to be yours.

There are going to be people who will convince you that you’re not doing enough, you’re behind, you don’t deserve to be in the room and it’s not true. Do what you want at the pace that works for you and everything else will follow.

I’m convinced that everything will work out; it’s just a matter of time. Give yourself grace and kindness, even if you don’t believe you deserve it.

title: Slang at state | Penn State references new students should know
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
For many incoming students at Penn State, your journey began the day you accepted your offer. However, truly feeling like a Penn Stater requires engaging with the traditions and slang that come with the campus.

Here’s a guide for new students — and those who may still be establishing their Penn State vocabulary — to campus slang you may hear this semester.

Sylly Week

At the start of every semester, professors around campus typically spend the first few days of class going over the syllabus and introducing content.

With a lighter course load, syllabus week, or “Sylly Week,” is an opportunity for students to spend time connecting with friends instead of focusing solely on classes. As it takes place before the stress of assignments and exams, students can relax and enjoy settling into campus life.

Meatball Mondays

McLanahan’s Downtown Market “Meatball Monday” has been a staple in students’ diets for over 15 years. With sandwiches being just $4.50, this weekly tradition offers an affordable, convenient and delicious meal option.

While the lines can be long, it’s a great way to beat the end-of-semester meal plan blues.

Willard Preacher

Arguably one of the most recognizable campus figures, Gary Cattell, better known as the Willard Preacher, is an essential part of campus culture. He can be found outside of the Willard Building on a nice day.

Almost always in a red shirt, the Willard Preacher can be seen sharing his own beliefs and engaging in debates with students about religion and culture.

After years of standing on the left side of the building, the Willard Preacher is a campus celebrity that generations of Penn State students will recognize during visits.

The BJC

The Bryce Jordan Center, commonly referred to as the BJC, is more than just a place to watch women's and men’s basketball. Hosting THON, sorority bid day and a variety of concerts, this arena is part of the spirit of Penn State.

With a variety of events and games, the BJC can easily be home to some of your favourite Penn State memories.

Frat Flu

While the name of this term may seem a little obvious, the origin of the virus continues to be a mystery. “Frat Flu” refers to the sickness that circulates campus at the beginning of every semester after students come back from break.

However, despite its name, the sickness spreads beyond just those who interact with fraternities. At a certain point in the semester, it may seem like you cannot walk into a classroom without hearing someone coughing.

The Bloop

As Penn State is a large campus, the CATABUS system is an integral part of trekking across State College quickly. The Blue Loop, referred to as “The Bloop,” is a route students can take to locations around campus and downtown.

Included in students’ tuition, “The Bloop” is an affordable way to get around campus and still make it to class on time.

Pollock Testing Center

Exactly as it says, the Pollock Testing Center is the location where professors across majors and colleges typically hold exams. With an essence of stress and despair, the center is located near South and Pollock dorms.

While the Pollock Testing Center may not be a favorite among students, it is a shared experience that all students have to conquer at some point.

THON

My personal favorite Penn State term is THON. A yearly tradition that symbolizes what it means to be a student here, THON is held every February in the BJC.

THON is the world's largest student-run philanthropy that aims to provide emotional and financial support to Four Diamonds families who have been impacted by childhood cancer.

THON provides a way for students to not only fundraise for an important cause but build friendships through its 17 committees.

FTK

As part of THON’s vocabulary, the term “FTK” stands for “For The Kids,” referring to the children and families that experience pediatric cancer every day.

At Penn State, students often use FTK as a reminder of why students spend hours fundraising and stand for almost two days straight.

MORE NSO CONTENT

title: Penn State women's volleyball earns commitment from libero Averi Bridges
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
The first addition of the 2028 class has been made.

Libero Averi Bridges announced her commitment to Penn State on the first day of contact for the class of 2028.

Bridges will join fellow Nittany Lion recruits on the 2026 U19 U.S. women's U19 national team. She joins middle blocker Taylor Harrington and hitters Olivia Henry and Nejari Crooks, all members of the 2027 recruiting class.

The 5-foot-8 libero becomes the first member of the 2028 recruiting class and ranks as the No. 34 player in the Prep Dig 2028 recruiting rankings.

title: State Theatre events from June 15 to June 21
author: Michelle Diamond | News Editor
description:
This week, State Theatre is offering a number of live events and movie showings for community members.

Ranging from children’s movies to an exploration of music, here's everything the State Theatre is hosting from June 15 to June 21.

Monday, June 15

Alicia Blue Live at The Attic

Having found success as a Los Angeles musician, Singer-songwriter Alicia Blue has built her music career from 2016 to now, combining a variety of styles.

Blue has explored Americana, folk and alt-country and is soon to record her debut country album with Shooter Jennings.

Her State Theatre performance will begin at 8 p.m. in The Attic.

Wednesday, June 17

“Dog Man”

In partnership with Schlow Library, State Theatre is featuring children’s movies on Wednesdays.

This week, the story of how a police officer and his dog became Dog Man, stopping villain Petey the Cat, will be told on the screen.

It begins at noon. Attendees must register on the State Theatre website.

“Gregg Allman: The Music of My Soul”

This documentary will tell the stories of Gregg Allman and The Allman Brothers Band through interviews and performance footage. The story will explore themes of racial division, fame and personal hardship.

The State Theatre will be showing it at 7:30 p.m.

Thursday, June 18

The Wood Brothers

Grammy Award-nominated folk group The Wood Brothers will perform at State Theatre on Thursday.

The group of three has performed across North America and is releasing their album “Puff of Smoke” on August 1.

North Carolina duo Viv & Riley will also be adding some indie style to The Wood Brothers’ performance.

Doors will open at 7 p.m., and the show begins at 8 p.m.

Friday, June 19

“Summer of Soul (...Or, When the Revolution Could Not be Televised)”

Ahmir “Questlove” Thompson’s debut documentary highlights the celebration of Black history and culture along with the capabilities of music at the Harlem Cultural Festival in 1969. Performance footage of Stevie Wonder, Nina Simone and more are featured in the award-winning film.

It will be shown at 8 p.m.

Saturday, June 20

“Men in Black”

Barry Sonnenfeld’s “Men in Black” explores intergalactic conspiracies and a top-secret agency, combining science fiction and action.

The showing will begin at 2:30 p.m.

Jim Lauderdale & The Game Changers

A Grammy Award winner and Nashville Songwriters Hall of Fame inductee, Jim Lauderdale, is bringing his bluegrass and country style to State College.

Six of his past albums were written and recorded with Grateful Dead Songwriter Robert Hunter.

Lauderdale’s 38th and 39th albums will be released this year, and he will be performing in The Attic at 8 p.m.

“Caddyshack”

This comedy by Harold Ramis and Brian Doyle-Murray takes place at a country club, with one of the caddies after a college scholarship and a win in the golf tournament.

This ‘80s comedic-sport film will be shown at 8 p.m.

Sunday, June 21

“First Blood”

Following a Vietnam vet who gets put in jail, “First Blood” explores themes of hate and revenge.

Ted Kotcheff’s thriller will begin at 7:30 p.m.

title: Penn State women's lacrosse adds former Stanford star Mallory Hasselbeck
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is revamping its coaching staff ahead of next season.

The Nittany Lions hired former Stanford attacker Mallory Hasselbeck as an assistant coach on Monday, adding to coach Kayla Treanor's staff.

Hasselbeck graduated from Stanford in 2026, where she started in 16 games. During her fifth year, she scored 25 goals and recorded 25 assists, being named to the Tewaaraton Award Watch List.

Prior to her time with the Cardinal, the Weston, Massachusetts, native spent four seasons at Boston College. While she only started one game, she tallied 17 oats and nine assists through her three seasons taking the field --- missing the 2024 season due to injury.

Her father, Matt, played 18 seasons in the NFL with four teams, throwing for 36,638 yards and 212 touchdowns.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State WR commit Jamir Dean flips pledge to Georgia
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another commit has flipped away from Penn State.

Jamir Dean flipped his commitment to Georgia on Monday, he announced via X.

Dean is the third player to flip from the Nittany Lions in the past week, joining cornerbacks Semajay Robinson and Zachary Gleason Jr.

Dean committed to Penn State as a 3-star receiver on May 1. Five days later, he received an offer from Georgia, was named a 4-star receiver and began being heavily recruited by the Bulldogs, including taking an official visit on May 29 to Athens.

During his official visit to Penn State on June 5, Dean was active on social media, recruiting several targets for Penn State.

Despite this, he decommitted just 10 days later, leaving the Nittany Lions with just Landon Blume at wide receiver in the 2027 class. Meanwhile, he becomes the second receiver on Georgia's squad, joining 3-star Taurean Rawlins.

Penn State is still targeting 4-star receivers Khalil Taylor and Deshawn Hall, but Dean's flip makes Taylor and Hall even more of a priority.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
On this week's episode of "The 107k Podcast," co-hosts Lexie Linderman, Andrew Deal and Noah Aberegg discuss the commitment flips of cornerbacks Zachary Gleason Jr. and Semajay Robinson and what they mean for Penn State's 2027 recruiting class moving forward.

They also talk about the Nittany Lions' newest commit, safety Caleb Cooper, and how important it was for them to get a second safety on board.

Finally, the crew rounds it out with some discussion about last weekend's official visitors and Penn State's momentum with Khalil Taylor and Deshawn Hall.

title: SEE IT: Former Penn State wrestler Bo Nickal earns first-round TKO at UFC Freedom 250
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion put on a show on the national stage.

Former Penn State wrestler Bo Nickal earned a first-round TKO over Kyle Daukaus at the UFC Freedom 250 event on the White House lawn.

Nickal advanced to 9-1 in his UFC career with the victory and finished the second bout of the night the same as the first on the card, a knockout. It was Nickal's second-straight knockout victory, winning his last bout on Nov. 15, 2025, over Rodolfo Vieira at UFC 322.

The former Nittany Lion is a three-time NCAA champion as part of Penn State's team from 2014-2019.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Hayley Kitching wins 800m silver medal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Hayley Kitching has further cemented herself among the best in Penn State history.

Kitching won silver in the women's 800-meters at the 2026 Outdoor NCAA Track and Field Championship with a time of 1:57.65.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native capped off her historic outdoor season on the biggest stage. During her 2026 season, Kitching broke her previous school record in 1:59.15, which she last set in 2024, and broke it again on Saturday by nearly two seconds.

Coming into Saturday's race, Kitching had run three straight sub-two-minute races, marking her first three times doing that outdoors.

During Thursday's prelims, Kitching won her heat in 1:59.46, but ultimately finished second to Arkansas' Sanu Jallow. The same happened in the finals, as Jallow broke the collegiate record in 1:56.85.

Kitching's medal marks the first 800m medal in Penn State women's history, as well as the first medal since 2018.

title: Penn State hockey draft profile | C Tessa Janecke’s historic career, consistency key to PWHL draft stock
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Tessa Janecke was already the greatest player in Penn State hockey history long before the moment she dropped the jaws of 5,176 roaring spectators in the third period of the 2026 Women’s Frozen Four against Wisconsin.

On home ice, the Nittany Lions were down, 3-2, to the defending national champions with less than five minutes remaining in regulation. Janecke cherry-picked and caught Nicole Hall’s head-man pass for a clear breakaway on her former Olympic teammate, goaltender Ava McNaughton.

Janecke, being trailed by defenseman Emma Venusio, faked a wrist shot that caused McNaughton to fall backwards, allowing Janecke to tie the game. It was her second goal of the game, and despite the Nittany Lions’ heartbreaking overtime loss in front of the home crowd, Janecke stole the spotlight once more for the final time in Happy Valley.

Now, Janecke is moving on to the next chapter of her hockey career: the 2026 PWHL Draft, where the best of the best in women’s hockey face off on the grandest stage the fledgling scene has ever seen. She has the makings of an early superstar in a rapidly expanding league.

Janecke’s legacy as Penn State hockey’s all-time leader in points — between both the men’s and women’s programs — and various program records across the board are testaments to her draft stock as one of the nation’s top players who can be the puzzle piece needed on any of the league’s 12 rosters.

“Those are not only huge moments for (Janecke), which we're proud of her for, but also, they put our program on the map,” Katelyn Roberts said. “She is such a talented player and we were so lucky to have her come here. It’s a testament to the amount of skill that our team has and also how much the program has changed under coach (Kampersal), especially over the past couple of years, with him being able to bring in that top-caliber talent.”

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Orangeville, Ill.

DOB: 5/12/2004

Height: 5-foot-8

Shot: Left

Career statistics

Goals: 89

Assists: 112

Points: 201

Shot percentage: .115

+/-: 112

Blocked shots: 125

Penalty mins: 223

Awards

Olympic Gold Medalist (Milano-Cortina 2026)

2x IIHF World Championship Gold Medalist

Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award Top-3 Finalist (2025-26)

3x Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award Top-10 Finalist

2x CCM/AHCA Second Team All-American

HCA National Rookie of the Year (2023)

USCHO All-Rookie Team (2023)

3x AHA Player of Year

3x AHA Forward of the Year

4x All-AHA First Team

AHA Tournament Most Outstanding Player (2025-26)

Projections

Projected round drafted: Early first round

Player comparison: Alina Müller

Scouting report

By the numbers and accolades alone, Janecke’s skill and importance to Penn State’s system goes without saying. Janecke set the program records in nearly every offensive category, had 17 multi-goal games, nine shorthanded goals and stood at the 1.39 point per game mark at the end of her storied tenure at Penn State.

She was also the program’s first top-3 finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award for the nation’s top player. Additionally, Janecke became the first Penn State athlete in school history to win a gold medal at a Winter Olympic Games.

The Orangeville, Illinois, native was the initial foundation that coach Jeff Kampersal built his team around, carefully curating an offense that complimented her two-way forward style in a way that went deeper than just her line with Katelyn Roberts and Abby Stonehouse.

Janecke is a dynamic threat from anywhere on the ice. Often more of a playmaker in college, she’s extremely talented at bringing out the best in her linemates. Her seeing-eye vision for making crisp, tightly-threaded passes often led to unexpected one-timers or tap-ins on the doorstep.

She’s also an efficient goal scorer, not simply because of the sheer amount of time on ice, but because of her mental ability to take quality shots; when the shot isn’t there, she looks for alternatives. At Penn State, she averaged 5.35 shots per game, the most in program history. But that figure could’ve easily been higher if she pounded the net like a shot-first minded forward.

Janecke, as she’d shown in the Frozen Four, also has the clutch factor that a franchise player requires. With the U.S. Olympic Team, Janecke filled the fourth-line center role but was inserted into the gold-medal game late in the third period. Her smart thinking, solid positioning and patience along the boards directly led to Hilary Knight’s game-tying goal to help the Americans beat Team Canada.

The year prior, Janecke etched her name into the hockey history books by scoring the overtime golden goal in the gold-medal game against Team Canada in the 2025 IIHF World Championships, proving her worth as one of the country’s most prominent rising stars.

Best team fit: Toronto Sceptres

Because Toronto elected to protect defensemen Renata Fast, Ella Shelton and goaltender Raygan Kirk, the Sceptres’ forward core is likely high on the list for the expansion teams to pick at with Daryl Watts already signed to PWHL Detroit.

That means that Toronto, who was already in need of center depth, is going to need quality forwards across the board. Janecke is able to provide in both of those categories and can be the player that the Sceptres build their new-look roster around. She has the potential to be the first-line center on a young and rebuilding team.

In addition, unless moved to an expansion team, she would reunite with Kiara Zanon. The pair were teammates at Penn State for the 2022-23 season when Janecke was a freshman before Zanon subsequently transferred to Ohio State.

Toronto would've held the fourth overall pick in the draft per the Gold Plan but was pushed to to the No. 8 pick by the four expansion teams. Should Janecke fall deeper into the first round, Janecke would be a no-brainer for general manager Gina Kingsbury to build a lineup that maintains consistency for the full 60 minutes.

title: Former Penn State RB Saquon Barkley named to inaugural Time100 Most Influential People in Sports list
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion and current NFL star has made a huge impact on the sports landscape in recent years, and he's been recognized for it.

Saquon Barkley was named to the inaugural Time100 Most Influential People in Sports list by Time Magazine on Friday. He was listed under the "icon" distinction.

Also among the "icons" are other household names such as NBA star LeBron James, MLB star Shohei Ohtani, and soccer star Lionel Messi. Other NFL stars included on the overall list are Jaxon Smith-Njigba, Myles Garrett, 2026 first-overall pick Fernando Mendoza and NFL commissioner Roger Goodell.

The running back finished his career at Penn State as the all-time leader in rushing touchdowns and total touchdowns, although his records have since been broken by Nick Singleton. He was later selected No. 2 overall in the 2018 NFL Draft by the New York Giants where he went on to win Offensive Rookie of the Year.

Barkley's influence largely comes from his time with the Philadelphia Eagles. He made an immense resurgence in the NFL when the Eagles stampeded to victory in Super Bowl LIX. In that season, Barkley became the ninth player in league history to hit the 2,000-yard mark and had a career-high 13 rushing touchdowns.

As a result of his incredible 2024-25 season, Barkley was named the 2024 Offensive Player of the Year and was featured as the cover athlete of Madden NFL 26.

Currently, Barkley is going into his ninth NFL season with 8,356 rushing yards and 55 rushing touchdowns. He's added 2,651 receiving yards for 16 touchdowns through the air so far.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: A guide to former Penn State wrestler Bo Nickal’s fight at UFC Freedom 250, key storylines
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The most historic sporting event of the year is almost underway, competing for headlines alongside the always star-filled World Cup.

Set for Sunday, UFC Freedom 250 is a highly publicized mixed martial arts card on the White House lawn, consisting of a press conference on Friday at the steps of the Lincoln Memorial and massive fan amenities. The spectacle marks a lot of firsts in the midst of a summer celebrating America’s semiquincentennial and comes with all the hype.

The fireworks and showmanship at the nation’s capital are already underway, and it’ll likely continue when action starts on Sunday, with fight bonuses raised from $50k to $400k in an attempt to incentivize a show. However, across the seven total scheduled fights on the card, there’s one specifically that hits Happy Valley.

One of the fighters with the most at stake once the bell rings is Penn State wrestling legend Bo Nickal, who will be taking on Kyle Daukaus in the middleweight card for his tenth professional MMA fight. Both fighters made weight Saturday and the stage is on for the biggest fights of their careers.

Let’s glance at the outlook entering the occasion.

Tale of the tape - Bo Nickal vs. Kyle Daukaus

NickalDaukaus Height 6' 1" 6' 2" Reach 76 in 76 in Age 30 33 Record 8-1 17-4Last Five Fights WLWWW WWWWW Fighting out of State College, PA Philadelphia, PA

Nickal’s recent history

For a three-time NCAA Division I champion, Nickal’s growth in the UFC has been a bit slower than originally intended. Nickal was one of the sport’s most hyped prospects given his collegiate history as one of the most high-profile wrestlers to switch to MMA, and he’s consistently been given the fast track to stardom.

“I wouldn’t really say that I’ve had a schedule,” Nickal said at the pre-fight press conference. “I feel like I’m grateful for all the opportunities I’ve been given to compete at big events…It’s a blessing, want to make the most of it.”

Nickal is a public friend of President Donald Trump, who will be in attendance and celebrating his 80th birthday on June 14th. A majority of Nickal’s fights throughout his career had been against other up-and-comers, yet this card won’t be his first in the spotlight.

The southpaw was selected to the undercards of UFC 300 and MMA legend Jon Jones’ return against Stipe Miocic at Madison Square Garden, to some fans’ dismay. But since then, Nickal has added a pair of fights to his record against more respected opponents. He faced veterans of MMA, Paul Craig and Reinier de Ridder, the latter of which handed Nickal his first loss via TKO.

“Bo’s a very talented guy. He’s got a high caliber of wrestling that obviously you need to be weary of; he hasn’t shown like a ton of it in his fights.” Daukaus said at his pre-fight press conference. “I feel like he wants to get up there and strike and showcase both of his skills and show that he’s very well-rounded … I’m just prepared for the best Bo Nickal I can get on Sunday.”

Yet those two tests for Nickal stood over a year ago; his last step in the cage was on Nov. 15, 2025, in which he stopped Rodolfo Vieira in Round 3 for his second UFC KO. Nickal is still early in his UFC career, but if he wants to make a name for himself quickly, this’ll be the perfect stage, as most in Vegas predict him as a heavy favorite to win by either knockout or decision.

Daukaus’ recent history

Kyle ‘The D’Arce Knight’ Daukaus arrives in the nation’s capital with a much different history in MMA than Nickal, as a solidified career fighter with a background in Jiu-Jitsu. With these grappling skills, 71 percent of Daukaus’ wins have come by submission. Also a southpaw, this is Daukaus’ second UFC contract after a seven-fight stint from 2020 to 2022.

“It says a lot to be able to make it back into the UFC after being cut, so (Daukaus is) a tough guy, got a lot of good skills,” Nickal said at pre-fight media.

Since rejoining the big leagues, Daukaus faced two other experienced fighters, Michel Pereira and Gerald Meerschaert, both of whom he finished in the first round.

“I think his first run in the UFC, he maybe looked a little tentative, but he was also fighting much better guys,” Nickal said when asked about Daukaus past at pre-fight media. “I don’t really think he’s beaten a lot of high-level guys. These last two fights, he beat tougher guys that are veterans, but it was also very quick. So, since he’s been back, he's only spent a couple of minutes in the octagon. I feel like I don’t really care what he’s doing, I’m ready to go.”

Daukaus has also been out of action since Nov. 2025, after pulling out of a scheduled fight against Vicente Luque in April due to being selected for this event. Training since high school, Daukaus is capable in almost every aspect of MMA and could pose a real tough enigma to crack for Nickal. Daukaus stands as one of the fighters on this card with the most to gain with a win, which is a real possibility given his recent form.

Other key fights

Besides the Penn State ties at UFC Freedom 250, the card also appears to be an MMA fan’s dream. With seven total fights, there’s almost no fat on the card, only real action that’ll have fans glued to their screens. One of the headlining acts of Ilia Topuria vs. Justin Gaethje or Alex Pereira vs. Ciryl Gane are almost certain to end with a knockout for one of their respective championship belts.

Josh Hokit, who is one of the UFC’s most viral rising stars with his new ‘Incredible Hok” character, was a late addition to the card with a heavyweight bout against Derrick Lewis, who President Trump personally lobbied to be added to the event.

As for Hokit, just like Nickal, he’s an experienced DI wrestler in his own right. The former two sport athlete, who also played football, with Fresno State faced Nickal on the mat in 2019 at the NCAA 197lb Championships, where he was dominated by the Penn State icon.

“Man, anybody normal, I’m hitting that double over trip … two months of training, I thought I could beat one of the best college wrestlers of all time,” Hokit said in a TikTok posted to the UFC on Paramount page, “I really went into that match thinking I could win.”

Filling out the events, there’s former bantamweight champion Sean O’Malley vs. Aiemann Zahabi and Mauricio Ruffy vs. Michael Chandler in the lightweight division. Kicking off the action for the night will be a featherweight bout between Diego Lopes and Steve Garcia.

How to watch

There’s no pay-per-view required to watch UFC Freedom 250; all that is needed is a basic Paramount+ subscription. Lopes vs. Garcia is set to kick off at 8 p.m. EST, with Nickal’s fight scheduled to follow after its conclusion.

Entry to the event is limited, exclusive to service members and high-profile figures. Yet, visitors can still experience the action in Washington D.C. from the UFC Fan Fest and Watch Party set up nearby at the park, The Ellipse.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State hockey draft profile | G Katie DeSa brings aggressive goaltending, fundamental structure
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was a largely untested team going into the 2026 NCAA Tournament, playing in the noticeably weaker AHA conference and having previously fallen to notable blueblood programs like Ohio State and Northeastern. The same could’ve been said about goaltender Katie DeSa, who despite leading the nation in shutouts, was one of the least tested goalies in the tournament.

However, in the NCAA Regional Finals, all questions were answered. DeSa had a stellar 36-save shutout in a neck-and-neck game until Penn State’s empty net goal punched its ticket to its program-first Frozen Four appearance. She proved that she’s an elite, professional-grade goalie although her regular season competition was relatively weaker than the rest.

From the moment she first stood between the pipes as Penn State’s starting netminder, the Pawcatuck, Connecticut, native has provided structure and reliability as the Nittany Lions’ last line of defense. She set the program records for career shutouts, winning percentage and goals-against average and was the second back-to-back AHA Goaltender of the Year in conference history.

A born winner, her presence and consistency is what aided Penn State in capturing four-consecutive AHA conference championships and pushed the blue and white to the Frozen Four. DeSa’s winning resume has much to offer both the old and expansion PWHL clubs as a potential franchise netminder.

“(DeSa’s) group has come in here and won and that’s the only thing that they’ve known to do,” coach Jeff Kampersal said.

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Pawcatuck, Conn.

DOB: 1/21/2004

Height: 5-foot-8

Catches: Left

Career statistics

Record: 64-13-3

Win percentage: 0.818

Shutouts: 25

Goals-against average: 1.43

Save percentage: .932

Awards

2x AHA Goaltender of the Year

2x HCA Goalie of the Year semifinalist

NCAA Shutouts Champion (2025-26)

2x All-AHA First Team

4x AHA Tournament Champion

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 4

Player comparison: Kristen Campbell

Scouting report

DeSa, standing at 5-foot-8, fits into the average height range by the PWHL’s standard, but with many elite goaltenders like Aerin Frankel and Maddie Rooney each at 5-foot-5, it isn’t her size that makes the biggest first impression — it’s her physicality.

Penn State’s ace between the pipes was notably one of the more aggressive goalies in the NCAA for the past three years, and used her frame to bully opponents who dared to encroach upon her blue paint. Particularly in the NCAA Tournament, DeSa constantly harassed her adversaries who tried to screen her.

It helps that defensemen are allowed to be more physical in the professional game, as the PWHL allows body checking unlike in college. DeSa’s up-and-at-them style aggression will fit right in with the league’s more physical defenders who will work in tandem to clear sight lanes.

Aside from her moxie, DeSa is a fundamentally sound netminder that cuts down angles by challenging the shooter and maintaining depth at the top of her crease to avoid top-corner shots over her shoulder. She’s even stronger in the butterfly, with the situational awareness and poise to not succumb to desperation scrambling.

While she can give promising rebounds for the opposition, typically off her pads, her positioning puts her in the position to make the second save, which is often more important than the first shot attempt. DeSa usually doesn’t have to move much; she finds her depth, works around the crease but doesn’t make any unnecessary movements.

There were times at Penn State where opponents shot specifically for rebounds and had presumably “golden” opportunities, but seemed to be routinely shut down by DeSa’s short adjustment.

Best team fit: PWHL Detroit

DeSa would be a great fit in Motor City’s debut goalie room because of who’s in charge and because PWHL Detroit didn’t sign a goaltender in Phase 2 of the expansion process.

Manon Rhéaume, the lone female hockey player to appear in an official NHL game and one of the greatest women’s hockey goalies in history, was hired as PWHL Detroit’s inaugural general manager. Her legacy and influence alone would lend to a potential goalie-first approach to building a franchise.

Couple that with Josh Sciba’s hire as the program’s first head coach, a former assistant coach for the New York Sirens as well as for the U.S. women’s national team that took home Olympic gold in Milano-Cortina, and DeSa will have a top-rate system to grow in her role as a professional.

Even should a seasoned netminder rank above DeSa in the initial depth chart coming off the expansion process, she’ll then have the opportunity to learn from someone who’s been in the league longer and understands how the professional game differs from college hockey.

title: Off the record | Sincerely Serena
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear Serena,

First off, I want to tell you that being in a relationship isn’t some defining quality you need in order to be satisfied with your life or yourself. I understand it might not feel this way, but you’re so much more than your relationship status, and anyone who says otherwise is lying.

For most people, seeking out a romantic relationship is inevitable, especially in college, and you’re going to meet someone who you think is everything and more. It’s so important you realize that rose colored glasses are incredibly deceiving.

The subtle red flags that look a light shade of pink and the stories you’ve purposely tuned out are the first sign that something’s wrong. Obviously, no one is perfect, but if the people who love and care about you are saying something is “off” or “a little sinister” (don’t ask), you need to listen.

I don’t mean taking everyone’s opinions at face value or siding with your friends over hearing out your partner. It’s all about balance. But remember, you don’t have to stay with someone who is constantly proving they don’t want you.

If your family, friends and even their friends aren’t in support or start telling you less-than-desirable things, take a step back and reassess.

Being able to prioritize your feelings, peace and, selfishly, your ego will save you from feelings of doubt. Don’t waste time on someone who doesn’t reciprocate the same energy.

Unfortunately, you might stay too long, but leaving late is better than never.

A relationship that’s thriving will feel right. And I know what you’re thinking: “What does right even feel like?”

Well, I can tell you what it doesn’t feel like. It doesn’t feel like changing yourself to be the person they think you are, and it certainly doesn’t mean sacrificing your morals and standards just to be able to say you’re in a relationship.

I think you should look for someone whom you truly enjoy being around, not someone you hope can come in and fix all the problems you perceive within yourself. (Cue Olivia Rodrigo’s “The Cure.”)

You should also have fun with them; you shouldn’t dread being in their presence or walk into a room and hear other people sigh. I once saw something that said, “Is he fun to be around, or am I fun and he’s just around?”

If your soulmate isn’t at Penn State, which I think might be likely, take pride in knowing that at the end of the day, you always have yourself.

A partner isn’t a substitute for the love you need to have for yourself. No one, no matter how great they are, can replace the self-worth you should possess internally. In fact, your romantic relationships should exacerbate the way you feel about yourself, and if they make you feel anything other than special, off they go.

Acknowledging that you aren’t in a space for a relationship is a sign of growth within itself.

You don’t need a partner to have “successful” college years, and you’ll be so much better off being single than being in a relationship with a loser.

You might be one of the lucky ones and find yourself in a relationship with someone who worships the ground you walk on, but if that’s not you, no worries. At the end of the day, you have amazing friends and, most importantly, the love you have for yourself.

I'm wishing you nothing but happiness in whichever way it presents itself.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: 40 year anniversary special | Which Penn Staters from the 1987 draft class had the best NFL careers?
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following Penn State’s 1986 national championship, a record 13 Nittany Lions were selected in the 1987 NFL Draft, with only one player not from the national championship team.

Despite the unprecedented figure, only a select few made an impact in the league, and success wasn’t necessarily based on where in the draft these players were chosen.

Here’s who had the most success in the NFL from Penn State’s 1987 draft class.

LB Shane Conlan

Linebacker Shane Conlan was the first former Nittany Lion selected in the 1987 NFL Draft, with the Buffalo Bills taking him eighth overall. He lived up to the expectations of an early first round selection.

In his rookie season, he made 114 tackles and was named the Defensive Rookie of the Year by the Associated Press. But that was only the beginning for the most decorated player from Penn State in this draft class.

The following three seasons in Buffalo were equally as successful, as Conlan enjoyed three consecutive Pro Bowl appearances from 1988-90. In the 1988 and 1990 seasons, Conlan was named a second team All-Pro.

From 1990-92, Conlan appeared in three of Buffalo’s four straight Super Bowl appearances, but failed to take home the coveted Lombardi Trophy. He was signed by the Los Angeles Rams for the 1993 season and retired in 1995.

Conlan finished his NFL career with 791 tackles, seven sacks, five interceptions and five forced fumbles, all recovered by himself.

DE/DT Tim Johnson

Tim Johnson was a sixth-round pick by the Pittsburgh Steelers and spent a decade in the NFL, during which he became the only former Nittany Lion in the draft class to win a Super Bowl.

Johnson spent three seasons in Pittsburgh before being picked up by the Washington Redskins. With the Redskins, Johnson won Super Bowl XXVI against Conlan and the Bills in 1991.

The 1991-92 Redskins are widely regarded as one of the best NFL teams ever assembled, and Johnson contributed to that with 82 tackles, a forced fumble and an interception.

Johnson played his last season with the Cincinnati Bengals in 1996, starting in 13 out of 14 games played and recording 41 tackles.

He finished with 480 tackles, six forced fumbles with four recoveries and an interception in 146 career games played.

RB Steve Smith

Running back Steve Smith is a good example of how a player doesn’t need to be drafted early to be impactful.

Smith was a third round pick to the Los Angeles Raiders, going just one pick after teammate and fellow running back Tim Manoa in the draft — Smith played 133 career games, more than D.J. Dozier and Manoa combined.

Smith retired in 1995 after seven seasons with the Raiders and two in Seattle due to a back injury.

Smith primarily played fullback in the NFL, blocking for backs like Bo Jackson and Hall of Famer Marcus Allen, but still produced 1,670 rushing yards for nine rushing touchdowns along with 1,264 receiving yards for 13 touchdowns by the end of his career.

After his rookie season was cut short by a knee-ankle injury, Smith’s best season was in 1988, recording three rushing touchdowns and six receiving touchdowns for 461 all-purpose yards.

His season-best yardage was the following year in 1989, when he put up 471 rushing yards — though he only scored a single rushing touchdown.

RB D.J. Dozier

Dozier was the second Penn State player selected in the draft, going 14th overall by the Minnesota Vikings. But he didn’t reach the same heights as his first-round counterpart Conlan; Dozier’s five years in the NFL burned bright, but faded just as fast.

In his rookie season, Dozier rushed for 298 yards and six touchdowns as the backup, fumbling the ball twice. He also had 89 receiving yards and a pair of touchdowns through the air.

In the following two seasons with the Vikings, Dozier put up similar figures in yardage but only scored two rushing touchdowns and no receiving touchdowns.

In the early 90s, Dozier was splitting his time between the NFL and Minor League Baseball. He was reactivated to the Vikings’ roster in 1990 and played six games, recording six rushing attempts for 12 yards. In 1991, Dozier once again saw minimal time after signing with the Detroit Lions off waivers, rushing for 48 yards on nine attempts.

Dozier retired from the NFL for good following the 1991 season with 745 rushing yards for eight touchdowns and 316 receiving yards with two touchdowns. He later made his MLB debut with the New York Mets in 1992.

title: Penn State wrestling's Connor Mirasola wins gold at U23 Pan-American Championships
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
With international competition underway, a Nittany Lion has already struck gold.

Connor Mirasola earned gold at 125 kilograms at the U23 Pan-American Championships.

Mirasola moved up to 125 kilograms, the heavyweight of the international weight classes and dominated a majority of his matches.

In his opening match, he narrowly defeated Mexico's Leonardo Guerrero Aviles 4-1 and then it was smooth sailing from there. Mirasola rattled off two tech falls over Puerto Rico's Ethan Vergara, 10-0, and Canada's Jorawar Dhinsa, 12-1, to pull to 3-0 and a chance at gold in his final match.

Mirasola defeated Colombia's Santiago Lozano Mantilla by a 10-0 tech fall to go undefeated on the day and earn gold.

It's the first time Mirasola has wrestled at this weight class, and it could be a sign of where he goes weight-wise for the folkstyle collegiate season.

title: Penn State baseball earns transfer from Clemson outfielder Bryce Clavon
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions just made their biggest addition of the offseason.

After earning a commitment from UMass' Anthony Tirado earlier in the day to push the incoming lineup additions to four, coach Mike Gambino and staff showed they weren't done yet.

A simple post, reposted by the official team, is all it took, yet Bryce Clavon will arrive in Happy Valley. He'll join with two seasons of baseball under his belt, the first in the SEC, where he had a brief appearance with Georgia, appearing in 27 games with only 15 at-bats. With the Bulldogs, he posted a .333 batting average and had a home run but struck out seven times.

After his freshman year, the Stockbridge, Georgia, native took his talents to Clemson, where those flashes landed him a full-time gig. Clavon appeared in 42 games with the Tigers, primarily in the outfield, in which he slashed .255/.364/.423 and had 18 RBI. This summer, Clavon is appearing in the Appalachian League with the Burlington Sock Puppets as their primary centerfielder. So far, he's played in only five games and has posted a .143 batting average.

Clavon arrives in Happy Valley with a solid resume, but a lot more to prove. Out of high school, he was the No. 51 prospect in the nation according to Perfect Game, which also gave him the highest possible grade.

He isn't just an outfielder; Clavon possesses the ability to play shortstop, with his specialty being his arm strength, as he even played QB in high school and held multiple DI offers. Perfect Game also rated Clavon as the No. 92 preseason prospect for the 2027 MLB Draft. Now, he'll potentially hear his name called for the pros and spend his collegiate future as a member of the Nittany Lions.
