title: A beginner's guide to the 2026 FIFA World Cup
author: Elliott Inscho | Sports Editor
description:
The FIFA World Cup is here, bringing with it more than a month of nonstop soccer action.

Hosted by the United States, Canada and Mexico, the 2026 iteration of the tournament will have a record-breaking 48 teams compete for the most coveted trophy in sports.

The World Cup is also the most-watched sporting event in the world during the years it takes place, so here’s a guide to get you up to speed.

Tournament format

Thursday’s opener between Mexico and South Africa marked the start of the group stage, a round where the four-dozen squads are sorted into 12 groups of four. Each nation inside a group will play three matches round-robin style against its groupmates.

Teams will receive three points for a group-stage win and one point for a draw. After all matches have been played in the first round, the top two nations in each group will automatically advance to the single-elimination knockout rounds, while the eight best third-place squads will also advance.

Once the groups are in order, the 32 advancing teams will be sorted into a traditional tournament-style bracket. From there, each round will halve the remaining teams until there’s just one left standing.

The final

If the World Cup is the most-watched tournament in the world, then the World Cup final is the most-watched game in the world.

This year’s title match will take place on July 19 at Metlife Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey. It won’t be the first major soccer match hosted at the home of the New York Jets and New York Giants; the FIFA Club World Cup final was played there last year, too.

World Cup history

The tournament began in 1930, taking place in Uruguay. The hosts claimed the first title with a 4-2 win over Argentina in the final. Since then, the trophy has been awarded every four years, except for 1942 and 1946 when the tournament wasn’t played due to World War II.

Brazil is the most successful nation in World Cup history, taking the prize five times. Italy and Germany follow close behind with four titles each.

Argentina, a three-time champion, won the last tournament in 2022. All-time great Lionel Messi brought his squad to the final in Qatar, winning the final in penalties after a 3-3 draw with France.

USMNT’s search for glory

After being eliminated by the Netherlands in Qatar in 2022, the United States will look to make some noise on their home turf.

The Americans took third place in 1930, but haven’t bested that mark since. They’ve been eliminated in the Round of 16 in three of the last four tournaments, although they didn’t qualify in the first place in 2018.

Star forward Christian Pulisic, a Hershey, Pennsylvania, native, will make his second World Cup appearance when the squad faces off with Paraguay on Friday at 9 p.m.

Other star Americans include forward Tim Weah, who scored against Wales in the 2022 tournament, and midfielders Weston McKennie and Tyler Adams, who have appeared in a combined 120 matches for the United States.

Players to watch

Kylian Mbappé, F, France

Mbappé is coming off of a hat trick in the 2022 World Cup final against Argentina, and is likely to be the biggest weapon on a deadly France side.

2026 will be Mbappé’s third World Cup. He won the title in 2018, becoming the first teenager to score in a World Cup final since Pelé.

Erling Haaland, F, Norway

Norway benefited greatly from this year’s expansion to 48 teams, making their first appearance since 1998.

Haaland is one of the world’s deadliest strikers, scoring 35 goals for Manchester City between the Premier League and Champions League last season. He’ll anchor a team of World Cup first-timers, and will face off with Mbappé and France during a group stage match on June 26.

Lamine Yamal, F, Spain

Exploding onto the international stage as a 16-year old at the 2024 European Championship, the now 18-year old Yamal is one of the most exciting players in the sport. He’s known for his strong passing and playmaking ability, but can fire a ball into the net with the best of them.

World Cup tunes

The official anthem of the 2026 tournament is “DNA,” a collaboration between global superstars Andrea Bocelli, David Guetta, EJAE and Megan Thee Stallion, but that isn’t the only song that will be flooding social media throughout the next month

Internet Streamer IShowSpeed, whose 2022 song “World Cup” garnered over 300 million streams between YouTube and Spotify, released a new song for this year’s tournament.

The similarly titled “World Cup (Champions)” has already amassed 42 million YouTube views, versus the official anthem’s 412,000.

How to watch

Most World Cup matches will be aired on Fox and FS1, and fans can stream all 104 games on Fox One.

Matches will be played across the three host countries, with tickets available on the FIFA Tickets Portal. Official tournament merchandise is also available on its site.

After their first meeting with Paraguay on Friday, the United States will take on Australia on June 19 at 3 p.m. Then, they’ll wrap up group stage play against Türkiye on June 25 at 10 p.m.

title: Penn State's Benjamin Brahmer, Marcus Neal Jr. named Phil Steele Preseason All-Americans
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Two Penn Staters have received national recognition.

Tight end Benjamin Brahmer and safety Marcus Neal Jr. have been named Phil Steele Preseason All-Americans. Brahmer was named to the third team, while Neal earned fourth team honors.

Brahmer is coming off a 37-catch, 446-yard season with Iowa State, where he hauled in six touchdown receptions. With the familiarity in the offensive system and the already strong chemistry with Rocco Becht, Brahmer could be in for a big season as the team's lead tight end.

Neal had a strong season for the Cyclones, where he tied for the team lead in tackles with 77 while playing safety. His playmaking ability could get him high recognition following a season where he forced three turnovers, including two interceptions.

While there's uncertainty on how the Nittany Lions will play in the first year under Matt Campbell, Brahmer and Neal bring familiarity to the system and strong seasons that they could build off to have even bigger years in Happy Valley.

title: Penn State women’s volleyball coach Katie Schumacher-Cawley named to Pennsylvania Volleyball Coaches Association’s 2026 Hall of Fame Class
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions’ third-ever boss has been immortalized.

After four seasons as head coach of Penn State, in which the program saw a rejuvenation back to national prominence during her tenure, Katie Schumacher-Cawley added some personal glory.

The blue and white boss was announced as a member of the PVCA’s 2026 Hall of Fame Class alongside advocate for the sport Dan Jackson and Juniata College coach Heather Pavlik.

Pavlik guided her own school to an incredibly successful period, and is the wife of longtime Penn State men’s volleyball head coach Mark Pavlik, who retired in 2026 after three decades in Happy Valley.

The latest inductee for the blue and white, Schumacher-Cawley, has established the program as a national contender and made good work on filling the shoes of the program’s longest-ever tenured and most successful head coach, Russ Rose, who retired in 2022 and is an AVCA Hall of Famer.

Penn State’s first-ever coach, Tom Tait, is also immortalized alongside Rose, meaning every single season of the program was helmed by a Hall of Fame coach.

Before landing the top gig, Schumacher Cawley worked as an assistant under Rose and held head-coaching jobs at UIC and Penn.

However, during her tenure in charge in Happy Valley, Schumacher-Cawley became the second head of the program to win a Big Ten Coach of the Year award in 2024. That same season, the Nittany Lions seized their eighth National Championship, becoming the first women’s coach to helm a title-winning team in the process and obtaining her second in her career after winning in 1999 as a player.

Schumacher-Cawley’s record as a coach in Happy Valley, however, stands at 103-32, with the winningest season in this millennium posted under her tutelage, along with only one year where Penn State finished with double-digit losses.

That remarkable season in which she reached the pinnacle of the sport during her public battle with breast cancer culminated in Schumacher-Cawley earning the 2025 Jimmy V Award and delivering a speech which gave Penn State one of its most iconic moments of all time.

Now, Schumacher-Cawley is immortalized forever for her work on the court and as a pillar of excellence through resilience.

title: Penn State baseball earns commitment from transfer UMass infielder Anthony Tirado
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Gambino has hit in the portal once again.

After losing a key batch of pitching and batting assets to transfers, the Nittany Lions have made their third offensive addition in the form of UMass infielder Anthony Tirado.

The Cheshire, Connecticut, native joins Fairfield outfielder TJ Schmalze and East Stroudsburg infielder Shanley Walls as Penn state's additions to its lineup so far.

Tirado had a stellar redshirt sophomore season with the Minutemen, in which he slashed .335/.403/.456 and had an improved second year of college baseball after a solid first season. He drove in 25 RBI with only a single home run in 2026, but wrecked the basepaths with 22 stolen bags.

In over 92 collegiate games for Tirado, he’s primarily seen action in centerfield despite that infielder listing, as he’s also capable of playing across the left side. The 22-year-old comes to Happy Valley with two seasons played and two in eligibility.

title: Penn State commit Zachary Gleason Jr. flips to West Virginia
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has lost its second commit in the last two days.

Cornerback Zachary Gleason Jr. has flipped to West Virginia following his official visit to Happy Valley last weekend.

Gleason Jr. committed to the Nittany Lions on March 31, three days after fellow cornerback Semajay Robinson. Now, he follows Robinson away from Penn State a day after Robinson flipped to Virginia.

Gleason Jr. took his visit to West Virginia a week before he came to Penn State and is now heading back as the only cornerback commit for the Mountaineers.

title: Penn State track and field wraps up season at NCAA Championship
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three months of action with numerous school records and winning marks, Penn State took the track for the final meet of the season.

10 Nittany Lions competed at the NCAA Outdoor Track and Field Championship from Wednesday to Saturday in Eugene, Oregon, to cap off a historic season.

Here’s a look at how Penn State fared across each day of action.

Wednesday

Graduate student Collin Burkhart opened up the action on the West Coast Wednesday afternoon, earning Second Team All-American status after throwing 67.82 meters in the men’s hammer throw. His mark finished 13th overall and improved his No. 3 time in school history.

In the men’s 1,500-meter, junior Nick Sloff’s standout season came to a close as he finished 23rd overall, running 3:42.96.

Penn State’s struggles continued in the men’s 100-meter, as sophomore Ajani Dwyer recorded his worst time of the season, finishing in 12th place with a time of 10.18 seconds.

Things bounced back in the men’s 800-meter prelims, as two of Penn State’s three entries booked their tickets to the finals.

Senior Niko Schultz led the way with a time of 1:45.47, finishing third overall. Graduate student Allon Clay also made his way to Friday’s finals, finishing in 1:45.76 for seventh place — the last at-large bid.

Graduate student Yukichi Ishii capped off his Penn State career with a time of 1:48.13, finishing in 19th place in his second career outdoor championship.

Senior Ryan Henry wrapped up the day in the men's shot put, finishing as a First Team All-American. He threw for 19.65 meters for sixth place in the meet and fifth in school history, while Dylan Lambrecht threw 17.33 meters for 22nd overall.

Thursday

The Nittany Lions only had two competitors take the track on Thursday for the women’s side.

Senior Hayley Kitching kicked things off in the women’s 800m, qualifying for Saturday’s finals with a time of 1:59.46. She won her heat and finished second overall, running her third straight race under two minutes.

In the women's shot put, sophomore Sasha Garnett threw for 16.37 meters, finishing in 16th place.

Friday

The Nittany Lions had two entries into the men's finals on Friday, with Clay and Schultz taking the track for the men's 800m finals.

Following his Big Ten title, Clay capped off his collegiate career with a time of 1:44.98, finishing in fourth place. Meanwhile, Schultz finished just behind in sixth place, running 1:45.50.

Both athletes capped off the 2026 season with First Team All-American honors.

Saturday

Kitching capped off the 2026 season in the women's 800m finals, where she finished second in the nation.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native furthered her 800m school record for the third time this season, running 1;57.65.

title: After union election win, Penn State faculty say next step is to ‘rebalance power’
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Last month, 2,510 faculty members voted in favor of unionizing. As a contract negotiation process with university administration begins, many involved with the Penn State Faculty Alliance (PSFA) see the move as a step toward shared governance.

Prior to the May 14 election, Penn State was the only one of four state-related universities to not have a faculty union, according to the PSFA’s website. Unionization efforts have been developing over several years, and faculty filed for a union election in December 2025. The union is part of Service Employees International Union (SEIU) Local 668.

Shared governance would give faculty members more of a say in university decisions. PSFA Member Sascha Meinrath said faculty and graduate students working toward respective unions represent a wider push for this.

“You really do have this ethos permeating the campus of a strong desire to rebalance power between the workers here at this university, whether faculty or graduate students, and the university administration,” Meinrath said.

Meinrath was previously involved in unionization efforts at Yale University and University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign. He said Penn State will likely put up the most resistance to shared governance with faculty during the contract negotiation process.

“It is fully within their power to make this a graceful, painless contract negotiation that has traditionally not been the tactic utilized by most administrations,” Meinrath said. “Bendapudi and the administration have the opportunity to avoid that, and we'll just have to wait and see if they wish to do this on the up-and-up, or whether they are going to deploy the same tactical playbook as often has been done previously.”

William Cromar first became aware of unionization efforts following the denial of a faculty-supported COVID-19 vaccine mandate by Penn State in 2021. He then became more active with the PSFA in 2025 and helped organize the election.

Cromar, teaching professor of art at Penn State Abington served on the campus’ faculty senate in various roles. He said the PSFA allows faculty to approve or deny decisions made by Penn State’s administration, something the senate can’t do due to its advisory role.

“More and more I began to see, both at the university level and at the college level, a kind of either co-opting of the senate role or just outright sort of ignoring the advice that comes out of senate,” Cromar said, recalling how he saw administrators present “preordained” decisions. “This was useful for them because then they could get their rubber stamp and then they could say, ‘Oh, look, shared governance.’”

While the PSFA and faculty senate may make decisions that indirectly impact both of them, Cromar said one organization should not overtake the other.

He said the faculty senate does not intend to negotiate contracts on behalf of faculty, despite already having a say in promotions and tenure. He added the PSFA should not influence curriculum, even though such decisions may have an impact on faculty workloads.

“I think there is a complementary role that the two play together, but they really are designed to do different things. The key here to understanding is what those different things are,” Cromar said.

Julio Palma, who was among the first faculty members in 2020 to begin organizing what would become the PSFA, said having a union earlier would have allowed more equitable decisions with respect to faculty desires to be made.

This was something Palma, an associate professor of chemistry at Penn State Fayette and one of the campus’ faculty senators, didn’t see occur when the decision was made to close seven commonwealth campuses.

“I'm not trying to say that with the union we would have prevented the closures. What I'm trying to say is the way it's happened, it's happening so fast,” Palma said. “People are being treated terribly. There's no way this would have happened with a faculty union; they would have needed to listen to us.”

While Palma acknowledges the potential for conflict between the PSFA and Penn State administration when negotiating a union contract, he said he’s “ready to have dialogues,” and both sides should be ready, too.

“I am willing to get together, to work together and do that, but now they have to respect our faculty union. I expect them to be professional and fair and recognize that now we have a union, and we have a say,” Palma said.

title: Penn State women's hockey draft profile | F Mya Vaslet offers grit, dominance on ice
author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description:
Mya Vaslet donned the blue and white uniform on June 18, 2021, after winning top forward at the 2019 Canadian Women's U18 National Championship for Team Ontario Blue. Since then, the forward from Stittsville, Ontario, has spent her collegiate career in Pegula Ice Arena as a steady bottom-six forward.

She weathered a season-ending injury four games into what would’ve been her senior year in the 2024-25 season, when she went hard into the boards and injured her knee. A week later, she was brought back to the hospital with two blood clots. She was only supposed to be out a few weeks, but it escalated to the remaining length of the season.

She announced her return for a redshirt senior season on May 19, 2025.

After finishing out her fifth year with the Nittany Lions and heading to the Frozen Four for the first time in program history, the Canadian is headed to the 2026 PWHL Draft on Wednesday in Detroit. Vaslet is among 236 players globally, including seven Nittany Lions, that are eligible for the draft.

“I think just coming back from a season-ending injury is pretty emotional and obviously a little nerve-racking, but I was happy that we did get it,” Vaslet said on her opening goal for Penn State’s fourth consecutive AHA Championship win against Mercyhurst on March 7.

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Stittsville, Ontario

DOB: 4/6/04

Height: 5-foot-9

Shot: Left

Career stats

Games played: 149

Goals: 30

Assists: 30

Shooting percentage: .109

Awards

4x AHA Tournament Champion

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 7

Player comparison: Maggie Flaherty

Scouting report

Vaslet became known for her clutch shots to give the Nittany Lions a leg up during important games, scoring crucial goals in two of Penn State’s four-consecutive conference championship games. Most recently, she scored in the first period of the 2026 AHA Championship against Mercyhurst, giving the blue and white an early lead.

“I call (Vaslet) ‘M-Train’ but I’m probably going to start calling her ‘Big Game Vaslet’ because she scores big goals in championship games,” coach Jeff Kampersal said following Penn State’s AHA Championship win in March.

Vaslet’s biggest success was in her junior year, where she hit her collegiate high shooting percentage at 0.167, tripling the statistic from the previous year. Playing in all 38 games of the season, her patent for big goals shone through in Penn State’s second AHA Championship game against Mercyhurst in the 2023-24, where Vaslet’s single goal stole the 1-0 win for the Nittany Lions.

In the last game of her senior season against UConn, Vaslet scored with less than a minute left in the third period, widening the blue and white’s lead to a 4-2 win. Despite a positive projection into her senior year, the Stittsville, Ontario, native had her 2024-25 season cut short after just four games due to a knee injury and resigned to the bench as an alternate captain for the year.

Vaslet’s health continued to be an issue exactly a year later during Penn State’s matchup against Vermont in October of 2025, where she left the ice early with a hand injury. More injuries plagued Vaslet’s fifth season in Hockey Valley, when she missed the series against RIT in February.

As a player, Vaslet is a gritty forward that uses her size to bully defenders along the boards for possession and in front of the net in order to screen opposing goaltenders. Her grinding style has offered her opportunities to capitalize on rebounds and score “garbage goals” in and around the crease.

When she drives towards the net, she uses her size and speed to her advantage for leverage on smaller defensemen. Her long wingspan allows her to keep the puck out of reach as she maneuvers in the offensive zone.

In addition, she’s a two-way forward that hustles on defense for loose pucks and often takes the body while her former linemates, Nicole Hall and Matilde Fantin, took the puck up ice. She was a consistent threat on the penalty kill and efficiently managed both ends of the ice.

Best team fit: Minnesota Frost

After the Frost lost forward Britta Curl-Salemme to the new expansion team in Detroit, Vaslet would fill Minnesota’s vacuum in the roster. The land of 10,000 rinks would benefit from a physical power forward like Vaslet, being able to use her 5-foot-9 build to dominate the ice and keep possession, as well as remaining a clutch shooter.

With her proving that she can recover from injury and still produce, Vaslet could perform at the Frost’s level as they were back-to-back Walter Cup champions in 2024 and 2025 and playoff contenders this past season.

Her peak shooting percentage from her junior year landed at 0.167 and the positive indication from her quality season post-recovery reflects the capacity for her to keep riding the momentum of her continued improvement.

title: Staff predictions | Which Penn State players will be highest-rated in College Football 27?
author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
Penn State finished the year having four players rated in the 90s in College Football 26, but each of those stars has since departed for the NFL, leaving plenty of open spots at the top of the EA ranks.

Most of those new top-rated guys for Penn State will likely have worn burgundy and yellow last season, but will now be wearing blue and white in College Football 27 after an offseason full of roster turnover under new head coach Matt Campbell.

Here’s who we think will be the top five players for the Nittany Lions when the game drops on July 9.

Andrew Deal

1. Safety Jeremiah Cooper 91 overall

2. Quarterback Rocco Becht 89 overall

3. Linebacker Tony Rojas 89 overall

4. Running back Carson Hansen 88 overall

5. Tight end Benjamin Brahmer 87 overall

Jeremiah Cooper is the highest-rated returning player and with the move back to safety, he could get a slight bump in rating. Cooper finished last season as a 90 overall at Iowa State, but he was still at cornerback. Now with the move back to safety, Cooper could jump to a 91 overall in the preseason ratings.

Rocco Becht, Tony Rojas and Carson Hansen all ended at 88 overalls and will likely be around the same rating. Becht and Rojas are going to be full-time starters, while Hansen will likely be splitting time with James Peoples and Quinton Martin Jr.

Becht and Rojas are coming off major injuries, and being fully healthy should propel them both to 89 overalls.

Hansen, like Brahmer, will not be seeing all of the playing time when the offense is on the field. While Brahmer could be on the field alongside Andrew Rappleyea, there’s a chance they’ll be a strong duo together rather than a one-player unit for the season.

Because of this, Hansen won’t jump in rating but rather stay at an 88, while Brahmer will sit at an 87 overall as the team’s top receiving threat.

Andrew Mercer

1. Safety Jeremiah Cooper 92 overall

2. Linebacker Tony Rojas 90 overall

3. Running back Carson Hansen 89 overall

4. Quarterback Rocco Becht 88 overall

5. Running back James Peoples 88 overall

It’s hard to argue against Jeremiah Cooper being the highest-rated player in this year’s game due to the fact that he is the only player currently at Penn State that had a 90 overall in last year’s edition.

Terry Smith’s coaching and Big Ten competition allow for Cooper to refine his craft and gain an additional two overall points. Additionally, before his season-ending injury, he was projected to be a Day 2 selection in the 2026 NFL Draft, giving EA more reason to give him the benefit of the doubt.

Tony Rojas was rated an 88 overall last year and was trending upward before his season-ending injury. He’s expected to be Penn State’s top linebacker, and I have the feeling a 90-overall mark is in the cards.

Carson Hansen and Rocco Becht are two players that were rated 88 overall last year and probably won’t shift too dramatically for different reasons. Hansen was the No. 1 running back at Iowa State, but now he’s entered a more competitive room with James Peoples and third-string Quinton Martin Jr.

Becht, on the other hand, was the Cyclones’ starting quarterback and will continue that role at Penn State. However, he’s coming off injuries to both of his shoulders, so it would be a tough decision for EA to change his rating by launch day due to the uncertainty surrounding his situation.

Of the running backs, I’d expect Peoples to see a bigger jump from his prior 85 overall to match that of Hansen’s 88 last year. He was the third-string at Ohio State, but will be able to produce at an increased level as the likely backup. That prospect alone should have him in the high 80s.

Lexie Linderman

1. Safety Jeremiah Cooper, 91 overall

2. Quarterback Rocco Becht, 89 overall

3. Running back Carson Hansen, 88 overall

4. Linebacker Tony Rojas, 88 overall

5. Tight end Benjamin Brahmer, 87 overall

Jeremiah Cooper will be Penn State’s highest-rated player in College Football 27 as he makes the transition back to safety following a brief stint at cornerback and an ACL tear. And it’s a rating that’s warranted after stellar play at Iowa State, something that should carry over into Happy Valley.

It’s hard to envision Rocco Becht not getting a slight ratings boost after joining the Nittany Lions considering he finished at an 88 overall last season. I don’t expect that boost to be large, but he should comfortably be one of the three top players for Penn State in the game despite undergoing offseason shoulder surgeries.

Carson Hansen shouldn’t receive a drop in his overall rating, but I have a hard time seeing him get bumped up, especially with plenty of other running back talent at Penn State like Quinton Martin Jr. and James Peoples. Keeping him at an 88 overall seems like a fair decision for EA, and Hansen can always work his way up.

As for Tony Rojas, who also finished 2025 as an 88 overall — it’s complicated. Do I think Rojas is worthy of a rating in the 90s? Yes, I do. However, after playing just four games last year, there isn’t much reason to give him a higher overall rating, especially considering he’s coming off a torn ACL.

Benjamin Brahmer rounds out my top five after finishing last season as an 86 overall. That should get bumped up, even if it’s ever so slightly, after being the top tight end in the transfer portal and the Nittany Lions’ most lucrative get.

Noah Aberegg

1. Safety Jeremiah Cooper, 92 overall

2. Linebacker Tony Rojas, 90 overall

3. Quarterback Rocco Becht, 89 overall

4. Safety Marcus Neal Jr., 88 overall

5. Running back Carson Hansen, 88 overall

Without a doubt, Jeremiah Cooper will be the highest rated player for Penn State in College Football 27 after finishing as the player rated in the 90s last season. While he’s recovering from a torn ACL when he played cornerback last season, his performance through four years at Iowa State warrants the high rating.

Also recovering from a torn ACL is Tony Rojas, who’s been a difference maker for the Nittany Lions over the past two seasons. After finishing as an 88 overall linebacker in last year’s game, his 25 tackles, two sacks and four tackles for loss through four games should boost him up to the 90 overall club.

Quarterback Rocco Becht was also an 88 overall player last season, and while he’ll likely see a slight improvement in his rating, the exact jump remains in limbo as he recovers from shoulder injuries and takes the reins in a new program.

Safety Marcus Neal Jr. is expected to make a big jump from last season’s 81 overall rating after finishing as one of the best defenders with Iowa State. While he’ll likely be the No. 2 defensive back on Penn State’s roster behind Cooper, he’ll still be one of the top-rated players in College Football 27.

Rounding out the pack is Carson Hansen, who finished last season at 88 overall. He’s projected to be the starting running back over James Peoples and Quinton Martin Jr., but may not see much of an improvement in his ratings due to splitting carries.

title: Penn State women’s hockey draft profile | D Kendall Butze defines playmaker, displays elite vision
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Following the first-ever NCAA Division 1 hockey game played inside Beaver Stadium on January 30, Kendall Butze continued to make program history against Robert Morris in Game 2.

The defenseman earned her record-breaking 85th career point after dishing a pass to Danica Maynard to record the fourth goal of the game. Butze continued to make a lasting impact throughout the season, setting a new program career-point record for a defenseman with 94.

Butze played all four years at Penn State, earning an alternate captain role as she donned the “A” in her final season.

The Cleveland, Ohio, native contributed throughout her final year for the Nittany Lions, finishing with a career-high 28 assists. This stat doesn’t come as a surprise, as Butze showcased her ability to set up plays and find open linemates for possible scoring chances throughout her time at Penn State. Her radiant energy and willingness to compete was evident from her freshman to senior year, ultimately translating into a dangerous weapon on the blue line.

“I talked about her a bunch, but she has pure joy when she plays,” coach Jeff Kampersal said. “(Butze) will play until she’s 90 years old, I can predict that. She just loves to play.”

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Cleveland, Ohio

DOB: 8/3/2003

Height: 5-foot-3

Shot: Left

Position: Defenseman

Career statistics

Games played: 151

Goals: 15 (6 PPG, 2 GWG)

Assists: 79

+/-: 96

Blocked shots: 155

Penalty minutes: 24

Awards

AHA All-Rookie Team (2022-23)

2x AHA Best Defenseman

2x All-AHA First Team

AHA All-Tournament Team

4x AHA Tournament Champion

Projections

Projected round drafted: 2nd round

Player comparison: Erin Ambrose

Scouting report

Butze has the majority of traits professional teams look for in a defenseman. She plays a 200-foot game and doesn’t take a shift off. Every time she is on the ice, her name is called. And whether it’s on the backcheck or forecheck, Butze doesn’t hesitate to show her speed on the ice. She can quickly join on the rush and instantly race back into position when facing an attack.

Paired with her wheels, Butze has remarkable vision matched with a high hockey IQ. She sets up scoring chances through her pass-first mentality. However, if she finds an open lane to shoot she won’t make a mistake. Whether it’s from the blue line or the slot, Butze has a precise wrist shot and sharp snap shot that can end up in the back of the net in an instant.

It doesn’t matter if it's for a tip-in or a snipe from the point, the defenseman chooses where she wants to put the puck and it goes there seemingly every time. Her head is up every time she has possession, leading to her smart decision-making.

Defensively, Butze continues to show her elusive skating by keeping the opposing rush in front of her and suffocating the puck carrier, forcing turnovers. Being 5-foot-3, Butze has shown signs of weakness when it comes to physicality. However, her positioning and glue-like pressure often become too much for attackers, making up for the lack of aggression.

Best team fit: New York Sirens

New York lacks depth, primarily in its defensive unit. After only protecting three forwards from the new expansion teams, the Sirens are thin in the pairings.

With Sarah Fillier and Casey O’Brien leading a young, promising offense, defense becomes a top priority for the Sirens heading into the draft.

Enter Butze, a young and hungry defenseman who can learn from the likes of captain Micah Zandee-Hart on the blue line. With its disappointing 83 goals against last season, New York will look for defensive help early in the draft and may go to the well twice in the position for added help.

If that is the case, Butze will likely find herself on the third pairing as a right defenseman alongside a fellow young defender. With the possibility of the Sirens going defense-heavy in the draft, a younger unit will spend the season learning behind the older pairings.

title: What to expect for upcoming IRONMAN triathlon, Pro Series in State College
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
State College is set to host the annual IRONMAN 70.3 Pennsylvania Happy Valley North America Championship triathlon on Sunday.

Up to 1,700 total athletes representing 43 countries and 44 U.S. states will participate in the triathlon, which started in 2023. It will also run alongside the third annual Experience Oman IRONMAN Pro Series, marking the first time Happy Valley has hosted the event.

Athletes will take a 70.3-mile course, including a 1.2-mile swim in Foster Joseph Sayers Reservoir at Bald Eagle State Park; a 56-mile bike ride through Centre and Clinton Counties; and a 13.1-mile run in University Park, finishing at Beaver Stadium.

Parking, traffic

IRONMAN Village parking will be open at 4 a.m. on Sunday in Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium and Lot 18 - Stadium West Lot, Beaver Stadium.

Transition one parking will be open at 4:30 to 7:15 a.m. on Sunday at Bald Eagle State Park; vehicles can leave the lot around 7:15 a.m., and athlete drop-off will be available here.

Twelve campus lots will be closed on Sunday, including Brown B, Brown E, Orange G, Red H, Silver B, Silver J, Silver N, Yellow E, Yellow H (except the section near the Nittany Lion Softball Park), Yellow L, Yellow R and Lot 42.

More parking and traffic information impacts for campus, including partial lot closures, can be found here.

Traffic along roadways in Harris and College townships are expected to be impacted due to the biking section of IRONMAN, which takes place between 8:00 a.m. and 4:30 p.m. on Saturday.

A detailed overview of traffic impacts can be found here.

The triathlon will also be streamed live on the IRONMAN YouTube channel, among other channels.

Event schedule

Below are spectator-friendly events open to the public. The full event schedule can be found here.

Friday, June 12

2 p.m. to 7 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: IRONMAN Village, which includes PLAYTRI Triathlon Store & Tech Service Center; an information tent; IRONKIDS registration and packet pick-up; and VIP spectator credential pick-up4 to 4:30 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: Q&A with the race director6 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: Shake Out Run with Penn State TriClub and special guest

Saturday, June 13

9 a.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: IRONKIDS9 a.m. to 4 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: IRONMAN Village11 a.m., Bryce Jordan Center - 1855 Club: Experience Oman IRONMAN Pro Series Panel1 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: Women’s Panel with pro athlete Danielle Lewis, IRONMAN 70.3 North Carolina Race Director Sami Winter and IRONMAN World Championship’s oldest woman finisher Natalie Grabow

Sunday, June 14; Race Day

4:00 a.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Parking Lot at Beaver Stadium: Shuttle bus for athletes and spectators6:50 a.m., Bald Eagle State Park: Pro men race start7 a.m., Bald Eagle State Park: Pro women race start7:05 a.m., Bald Eagle State Park: Age group race start10:45 a.m., outside Beaver Stadium: Approximate time for first finisher8 a.m. to 4 p.m., Lot 41 - Jordan East Lot, Beaver Stadium: Official IRONMAN Store and Medal Engraving4:30 p.m., Lot 18 - Stadium West Parking Lot at Beaver Stadium: Awards ceremony and 2026 IRONMAN 70.3 World Championship slot allocation/rolldown ceremony

title: Former Penn State pitcher Harrison Lollin transfers to North Florida
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion has found his new home.

Pitcher Harrison Lollin has officially transferred to North Florida following his sophomore year with Penn State.

Lollin showed significant improvement from his freshman to sophomore season, dropping his ERA from 10.87 to 4.05. However, he made nine fewer appearances, making 11 appearances across 13.1 innings, recording one save.

The Monroe Township, New Jersey, native will have two years of eligibility remaining to play for North Florida in the Atlantic Sun. The Ospreys recorded a 31-24 performance in 2026, going 0-2 in the tournament to end their season.

title: ‘It’s a record-breaking Pride’ | Centre LGBT+, community members prepare for upcoming Pride celebration
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
While big cities like New York City and Washington D.C. gather over a million attendees at Pride parades and festivals, State College residents enjoy a smaller pride festival catered to the local community.

Centre LGBT+ will host its fifth Pride parade and festival on Saturday. Beginning at 10 a.m., vehicles and marchers will parade through downtown State College and convene at noon in the Sidney Friedman Park for its Pride festival.

Cat Cook, the executive director of Centre LGBT+, said this year’s parade is expected to have almost 1,200 marchers, hundreds more than it had in 2025. She called it a “record-breaking Pride,” and said people can expect more vehicles, floats, vendors and attendees than were in previous celebrations.

“The local community is what has been getting us through,” Cook said. “They really came through with support this year in making sure that we are able to bring this event, which has really become a staple to County.”

She said many people who have attended bigger Pride festivals prefer Pride in State College because of the “local feel.” The festival is less commercialized and many local nonprofits, places of faith and businesses come together to support the community.

This year, the festival’s grand marshal, a leadership role given to to honor contributions made to their community, is Downingtown Mayor Erica Deuso. Elected in November 2025, Deuso is Pennsylvania’s first openly transgender mayor.

Cook said Deuso, a transgender leader in a difficult political climate, fights for policies to protect dignity, equality and access to essential services. She said Mayor Deuso brings concerns directly into public office and ensures the LGBTQ+ community is not treated as an abstract issue, but as people with real lives.

“It's very important that our grand marshal is representative of the community that we serve, and so Mayor Erica Deuso is the epitome of that,” said Cook. “We felt that it was important to highlight her this year, and to really bring forward all that she does and stands for because that's a lot of what we do here in our community.”

Mayor Deuso will give her opening remarks at noon alongside Corinne Goodwin, the founder and executive director of Eastern PA Trans Equity Project and State College Mayor Ezra Nanes, before local musicians and drag performers entertain the audience.

Margot Bevington, an award-winning children’s musical artist and Centre LGBT+ youth program coordinator, said she feels lucky to share her message with the audience.

She said performances like this can feel vulnerable, but music is a great way to bring community together and reflect on the present moment.

Bevington will perform a music set in the Family Zone. This area will include face painting, balloons and a puppet lab with Adam Swartz Puppets. She will also perform her original song “Pretty Good Day” in honor of Renee Good and Alex Pretti; in January, amid a federal immigration crackdown in Minneapolis, Good was fatally shot by an Immigrations and Customs Enforcement agent, and Pretti was fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent.

Bevington said exposure to diversity can counteract fear-mongering about children being exposed to the LGBTQ+ community.

“To vilify someone just for being themselves is wrong,” Bevington said. “We’re all human.”

Ian Hill, executive assistant at Centre LGBT+, said the Pride festival is not exclusive to queer people, but rather for all ages, cultures, sexual orientations and gender identities.

He added that it's also important for allies to show up in support of the LGBTQ+ community, as there is strength in solidarity.

“We won't be silent about sticking up for people's rights, that we're here and we're not going away," said Hill. “Tactics that are used to inspire fear to get people to be quiet or to go away or to hide aren't going to work.”

title: Penn State women’s hockey draft profile | F Maddy Christian brings speed, quick release
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Heading into her first-ever Frozen Four appearance, Maddy Christian sat alongside her coach Jeff Kampersal and defenseman Kendall Butze as they spoke to the media. Although the uncertainty of making the National Championship was on her mind, Christian spoke about the sense of connection and appreciation she immediately felt upon joining the team her freshman year.

The Elk River, Minnesota, native was one of the core seniors who led Penn State through its historic season. While she didn’t break any records, Christian finished third on the team in goals with 19 and earned her career-high 35 points.

The forward, along with center Grace Outwater and winger Mikah Keller throughout the majority of the season, created one of the most consistent forward lines on the team. The chemistry shown from the three forwards was evident nearly every time they were on the ice together.

However, when it reached a point in the season Christian had never been a part of before, she didn’t owe the success to mere skill, but to the “culture” that contributed to her success in her Penn State career.

Now, Christian looks to bring both her skill set and great sense of unity to a PWHL team in need.

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Elk River, Minn.

DOB: 12/24/2003

Height: 5-foot-7

Shot: Left

Career statistics

Games played: 153

Goals: 46 (9 PPG, 11 GWG)

Assists: 38

+/-: 54

Blocked shots: 86

Penalty minutes: 76

Awards

All-AHA First Team

All-AHA Second Team

AHA Tournament MVP

4x AHA Tournament Champion

Projections

Projected round drafted: 5th round

Player comparison: Daryl Watts

Scouting report

There’s a reason why Christian earned the Most Outstanding Player award in the 2025 AHA Championship game. She had a two-point performance, but her consistency throughout the season leading up to the game shone through the most. Christian continued to prove to be a reliable weapon when her line was called.

Christian was one of Penn State’s ironmen after playing in all 39 games on the season. She shows relentless aggression and speed on the forecheck as one of the fastest skaters on the roster. Her speed and agility was a strategy Christian played into, often remarking that her speed is her primary offering to the team.

“We have the chemistry where (Keller’s) got great vision, (Outwater) shoots the puck, I have speed,” Christian said. “We all have our different attributes that make us so great.”

Whether it be setting her linemates up when entering the offensive zone or receiving a pass and sniping it from the slot, Christian doesn’t stop her attack unless she hears the horn or whistle. A main part of her game is finishing every play she’s a part of.

Additionally, she can become very creative with the puck on her stick. The forward can utilize her quick wrist shot or resort to her elite puck handling skills to deke past both the defenders and goalie.

Alongside her quick attack, Christian shows her high IQ in how she positions herself in the offensive zone. Either on the entry or stationed at the top of the crease for a redirect attempt, the forward knows where to be to find the back of the net.

An area of her game that hasn’t been so consistent is off the puck. When she is forced to defend in her zone, Christian can show signs of struggle when matched with an attack. At 5-foot-7, she can easily be protected off the puck and lose the physical battle more often than not. However, her offensive skill when in possession can make up for it quickly.

Christian may not be up there with the likes of Tessa Janecke and Abbey Murphy when it comes to offensive talent, but she’s still capable of developing into a reliable, bottom-six weapon in the pros.

Best team fit: Toronto Sceptres

Toronto was one of the more inconsistent teams over the past season. The offense can fire on all cylinders and put up a four-goal game, then come back the next day and not find the net once. It’s clear that the team needs depth in the forward position that can consistently finish plays, especially considering the loss of star forward Daryl Watts.

The Sceptres protected two defenseman and a goalie from the expansion teams: Renata Fast, Ella Shelton and Raygan Kirk. With that in mind, Christian will become one of the many forwards Toronto will look at throughout the draft. She would likely find herself in the bottom-six, however; could quickly work her way up into the top-six due to the Sceptres becoming thin in the forward group.

Defensively, Toronto only allowed 72 goals against on the season. A major part in that number was Kirk who was a stellar starter in net after posting a 1.87 goals against average in 23 games.

The Sceptres can quickly become a contending team with the additions of a few young forwards. Christian fits the mold they are looking for: a consistent, speedy winger that can set up and finish plays in the attacking zone.

title: Column | The side of Mexico not shown in media
author: Lailah Acuña I The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite my parents both advocating for me to choose Spain instead, I made the solo decision to go to Puebla, Mexico.

I got a mixture of mixed reactions from my friends and family. My friends joked with me about being kidnapped by the cartel. My mom’s friend said he was scared for my safety. And I remember my one friend making a comment wondering if I was going to be living in a “shack.”

“Are you there to do social work?” someone asked.

No.

The Mexico we see portrayed in the news and media is just one side of the country, and it happens to be the one that gets the most attention. In turn, we have created negative stereotypes of a country that, in reality, isn’t too different from our own.

That isn’t to say it’s completely the same or 100 percent safe — safety is always a given measure, and caution isn’t something I live without. Traveling to a new and unfamiliar place is dangerous in itself, especially when the news is inundated with stories of crime, drugs and corruption in that place.

But the truth is, since arriving here in Mexico, I haven’t felt any less safe than how I do in the States. I’ve taken Ubers several times alone to different parts of Puebla and to the university where I’m studying.

Puebla is a small, non-touristy part of Mexico, about two hours from Mexico City. The university is full of nature, and just like at Penn State, it has animals that wander the campus. (But instead of bunnies, it has ducks.) There’s a huge library, a cafeteria with different foods just like the HUB, and an entire building dedicated to engineering, sciences and craftsmanship.

It’s so cool! The classes and buildings at this university are even more modern than some of the buildings at Penn State.

Here, they also have tons of malls, like Angelpolis, which my friend and I go to frequently. It’s full of high-end fashion stores, along with some more casual options like Sears; and there's Liverpool, which is like a Mexican Macy’s or JCPenny.

Malls are so common here in Puebla that there’s about three others not too far from Angelpolis. They all have stores common in the States, along with other Mexican or foreign name brand stores. Writing about it already makes me want to go back and spend more, but I’ve already bought too much, so I shouldn’t!

And, my house isn’t a shack. It’s a house bigger than my own in a gated neighborhood. My room is huge and I sleep in a big comfortable bed. The only thing missing is AC — the one thing I really miss about the States.

While I can say I feel completely safe in Mexico, I don't want it to completely overshadow the truly dangerous and dark parts of this country. If you choose to travel somewhere, it is always important to research before you go.

But from my experience in the places I’ve visited so far, I haven’t encountered any drugs, cartels nor crime. I would recommend traveling to Mexico — whether it be Puebla, Oaxaca, Mexico City or Cholula.

So, yeah, there’s risks. And premade assumptions often ignite those dreadful what-if questions: What if I'll be in danger? What if it's like what I see on the news?

A simple answer is that both of those are unlikely. Mexico is beautiful, and I would return a hundred times over. People are friendly. They compliment my Spanish, they take interest in getting to know me. I’ve made local friends from the university in Puebla. The restaurants are phenomenal and so nicely decorated.

Best of all: my host family is so amazing and the hospitality from everyone is too.

So if you’re contemplating traveling to or studying abroad in Mexico, I urge you to do so. Traveling abroad is one of if not the best decisions I have made.

Not only has it given me the opportunity to experience another world and culture, but it has shown me another side of a beautiful country different from the violence so frequently portrayed in the media.

And that beautiful side is the one I know I’ll be remembering.

title: Penn State hockey draft profile | F Katelyn Roberts brings pro-level, power forward skill set
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Katelyn Roberts has been one of the stalwarts at Penn State in recent years, a four-year Nittany Lion who never missed a beat and was never limited to her time on the ice. The forward’s development followed the same chain of consistency, as she blossomed to averaging 30 points per season over her last three campaigns.

In her final collegiate year, Roberts earned the nod as an alternate captain for a Nittany Lions’ unit that pushed all the way to the Frozen Four.

Now, she joins a batch of seven former Nittany Lions looking to make a splash in the PWHL, and for Roberts, the outlook looks incredibly strong. Let’s recap the tale of the tape for Roberts with the draft a little over a week away.

“She’s a power forward, great shot, really elite passer,” coach Jeff Kampersal said after the regular season finale on Feb. 14. “Off the ice, she’s the greatest connector of all time. I’ve been coaching 30 years and she just brings everybody together. I can’t speak highly enough of her.”

Bio & Metrics

Hometown: Chanhassen, Minn.

DOB: 4/12/2004

Height: 5-foot-7

Shot: Right

Career statistics

Games played: 153

Goals: 43 (12 GWG, 10 PPG)

Assists: 60

+/-: 64

Penalty Mins: 48

Awards

All-AHA Second Team (2025-26)

All-AHA First Team (2024-25)

4x AHA Tournament Champion

Projections

Projected round drafted: Round 3

Player comparisons: Hayley Scamurra

Scouting report

During her collegiate years, she demonstrated a knack for being a true power forward, able to complement flashier, puck-moving players as an offensive piece.

She brings a scrappiness that should serve her well at the next level since that aspect of her game was limited in college, due to NCAA rules against checking. Roberts may be able to fully unlock her skillset in the PWHL, where those restrictions no longer apply, showcasing physical two-way abilities not previously seen.

This shouldn’t overshadow her offensive skills; Roberts is a player who can join a talented offense and help elevate it, and she is one of the most possession-heavy forwards in this draft class. Based on her Corsi rating, an advanced analytics that shows controlled possession to scoring chances, she posts a top-10 figure. When she touches the puck, she’s extremely efficient, but it isn’t just offensive capabilities which favor her in advanced analytics.

The Fenwick formula is a stat that shows, as a percentage, how much a player influences their team’s shots relative to their opponents'. Roberts posted a 66.2% Fenwick, which ranks No. 6 among all NCAA prospects, and shows that when on the ice, Penn State controlled play more.

Roberts likely won’t be a possession-heavy player when she initially joins the PWHL, but she will help control the flow of the game and seize momentum for what she does without the puck. While it’s easy to label her a classic two-way forward, she brings more to the table.

Her ceiling may be limited, but there is little she cannot do. Depending on coaching decisions, different versions of Roberts may present themselves. Her archetype will likely still be defined by her physicality, but as a bottom-six forward she should quickly help fill gaps.

With more than half of her career points coming from power-play and game-winning goals, she has proven herself in high-leverage situations. If she continues to move up the depth chart, she can form a formidable line with some of the game’s top young forwards.

Best team fit: New York Sirens or any expansion franchise

When it comes time for teams where Roberts could find a home, she’s a player who could slot into almost any team in the PWHL. Out of the existing franchises, however, the New York Sirens would be the optimal spot for her after their primary physical forward,

Taylor Girard missed almost all of last season due to injury and has a looming free agency set. New York showed last season that it can compete for the future, but it still has a few holes, with those gaps being exactly the kind of areas Roberts’ skill set can address.

The Sirens desperately need a complementary forward who can join their other young stars, Sarah Fililer, Casey O’Brien, and Kristýna Kaltounková. Roberts could feasibly slide in and make an immediate impact.

Roberts could be a key addition to any expansion franchise as well, as throughout her career, she’s shown to be a stellar teammate who’s able to buy into the culture and spread that within her teammates. For a new squad that’s going to be extremely important.

title: Penn State men's basketball releases jersey numbers for 2026-27 season
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After an offseason full of roster turnover, Penn State released jersey numbers for the 2026-27 season.

There's good news for those who've already bought an Ivan Jurić jersey last season, as he'll be wearing the same number in No. 3. Guard Reggie Grodin and walk-on Chris Lotito also have the same numbers from a year ago with No. 12 and No. 30, respectively.

Point guard Jay Rodgers will don No. 1, which is the same number he wore at Central Connecticut State. The Penn State No. 1 jersey was last worn by forward Mason Blackwood last season, and Ace Baldwin Jr. was the most recent point guard to wear the number for the Nittany Lions in 2024-25.

Wing Brant Byers is set to have No. 22, which is also the number he wore at Miami (Ohio) last season. Forward Saša Ciani last wore No. 22 during the 2025-26 season.

Guard Roberts Blums will wear No. 45 like he did with Davidson last year. He'll be the first Penn State player to wear that number since at least the 2006-07 season.

Center Tim Oboh will don No. 33 while Thomas Allard will wear No. 14 to round out the Nittany Lions' transfer additions. Incoming freshman Andy Gemao will have Dominick Stewart's former number in No. 7, and walk-on Grant Spacciapolli has Freddie Dilione V's former number in No. 5.

International addition Aleksandar Zecevic will wear No. 10, which was worn most recently by Josh Reed last season.

Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut are not included in this announcement as they've yet to sign with Penn State, but they are still committed. Summer practices for the Nittany Lions will begin next week as new players will arrive this weekend.

title: Hit-and-run injures former Penn State long snapper, kills his fiancee; man charged with seven felonies
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn State long snapper Kyle Vasey was seriously injured and his fiancee, Corinne More, was killed in a hit-and-run incident on June 1 in Colorado, according to CBS News.

Douglas County, Colorado police identified 28-year-old Adam Bauserman as the suspect and charged him with seven felonies, according to a statement on the sheriff office's website.

Another pedestrian, Diane Windes, was also injured in the crash. The statement said Windes and Vasey were transported to local hospitals.

Vasey and More were on a walk when they were struck by a vehicle on the sidewalk, according to a GoFundMe page created by Vasey’s parents, Shelley and Bruce Vasey, in support of his recovery.

“A pickup truck negligently drove off the road and ran them down, going approximately 45 mph. The driver turned around to check out the damage he caused, then cowardly fled the scene,” the page’s description read.

Witnesses said it appeared they were struck intentionally, according to CBS News.

Bauserman’s felony charges include vehicular homicide; failure to remain at the scene of an accident involving a death; assault in the second degree; two counts of failure to remain at the scene of an accident involving serious bodily injury; and two counts of vehicular assault. His misdemeanor is for driving with a license under restraint due to a 2025 DUI conviction.

His bond is set at $100,000, according to the sheriff's site, and his preliminary hearing is set for Friday at 9 a.m.

Vasey, a Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania, native played for Penn State from 2014 to 2018. As of Tuesday afternoon, the GoFundMe page has raised over $30,000.

More’s sister said she was “loved by everyone who knew her” and “beautiful inside and out,” according to CBS News.

The GoFundMe description said Kyle is currently on a ventilator after enduring fractures all over his body, including three spine fractures and four broken ribs, and he has had six surgeries.

His family is “optimistic” about his recovery, according to the description.

title: Penn State CB commit Semajay Robinson flips to Virginia
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s first commit in the 2027 class has flipped to another school.

Cornerback Semajay Robinson has flipped to Virginia after his official visit with the Nittany Lions, he announced via Instagram on Tuesday.

Robinson visited Virginia on May 29 and then visited Penn State a week later with a big group of his fellow commits. However, following the visit, Robinson decided to flip to the Cavaliers.

The Florida native ranks as the No. 501 player, No. 51 cornerback and No. 42 player in Florida, becoming Virginia’s highest-ranked commit in the 2027 class.

Penn State is left with three cornerbacks in the class after Robinson’s flip, while he becomes Virginia’s first corner commit in the upcoming signing class.

title: Film review | Penn State transfer Skylar Durley displays physical point guard abilities
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s been many years since Penn State has taken home top conference awards, but the Lady Lions added a conference award winner last week when former Nevada freshman Skylar Durley committed to the blue and white.

Durley spent the 2025-26 season with the Wolfpack, where she was named the Mountain West Freshman of the Year.

Now, she’ll serve as the newest member on coach Tanisha Wright’s squad and bring a lot of experience to Penn State after starting all 31 games last season. Durley averaged 11.8 points, 6 rebounds and 2.8 assists per game, shooting 40.5% from the field in her standout season.

Here’s a look at what Durley will bring to the Lady Lions during her first year in Happy Valley.

Physical drive

Despite standing just 5-foot-9, Durley possesses a lot of toughness and physicality when driving to the rim. Her attack echoes comparisons with former Lady Lion Ashley Owusu, who won the Ann Meyers Drysdale Award in 2021.

Coupled with her toughness is strong ball-handling ability and aggressive nature, allowing her to work her way through defenders en route to a score.

While some use their ability to drive as a way to draw fouls and get to the free throw line, the Oklahoma City native looks to finish each possession at the basket.

Against Utah State, Durley recorded a career-high 22 points. Of those points, 18 came from the field as she went 0-for-1 from deep and 4-for-10 from the free throw line. Instead, she put up her best scoring output and one of her most efficient nights at the basket, shooting 47.4%.

Playmaking opportunities

A place Penn State often struggled in recently was playmaking. Whether it was cold stretches or a lack of creativity, the Lady Lions often found themselves without any scoring opportunities.

Contrarily, Durley’s freshman of the year campaign brought many instances of strong playmaking abilities.

During times when she was the point guard, Durley made big plays with the ball in her hand, often needing little-to-no help from her teammates.

While Durley isn’t known as a strong deep shooter, she found some success on isolation opportunities when she was forced to make a play.

Fast-break success

Durley’s drive and playmaking abilities derive from her hustle, which has been seen as she makes her way from the 3-point line toward the basket for the score. However, she displays that same hustle on fast-break offense, moving from one baseline to the other.

After a steal by Durley’s teammate on one side of the court, Durley hustled down the court and found herself in an open position, receiving a pass and finding the fast-break score.

The 5-foot-9 guard has shown she can run the fast-break by herself. Against Utah State, she ran through traffic up the court and scored a midrange jumper, adding onto her historic day.

title: 10 Penn State track and field athletes to compete in NCAA Championship
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is coming off a dominant NCAA East First Round meet with numerous school records. Now, there’s one last meet standing between it and a potential national championship.

Ten Nittany Lions qualified for the NCAA Outdoor Track and Field Championships, set to take place from Wednesday to Saturday in Eugene, Oregon.

Here’s a look at each race Penn State will be a part of, and where the Nittany Lions stack up.

Men’s 800m

Penn State’s biggest race of the week comes in the men’s 800-meters, where three Nittany Lions will be taking the starting line at 9:58 p.m. on Wednesday.

Graduate student Allon Clay leads the way with the No. 2 mark in the nation, having ran 1:44.63 in the NCAA East First Round prelims to set the No. 3 time in school history. He’ll race in Heat 2 alongside Georgetown’s Tinoda Matsatsa and Texas A&M’s Peter Narumbe — both who have run under 1:45 this season.

Joining Clay in the second heat is graduate student Yukichi Ishii. The Yachiyo, Chiba, Japan, native has the No. 19 mark in the country and is seventh in his heat with a time of 1:46.48.

The final entry for Penn State is senior Niko Schultz, who has the No. 4 time in school history in 1:45.24. Schultz enters this week with the No. 14 mark in the country, but is racing against four sub-1:46 runners in the third heat.

The three Nittany Lions will look to qualify for the 800m finals at 9:14 p.m. on Friday.

Women’s 800m

Senior Hayley Kitching is coming off back-to-back school records in the prelims and finals at the NCAA East First Round, running sub-two minutes in each race.

She also enters this week’s competition as the No. 4 seed, but has a much steeper path to success. Arkansas’ Sanu Jallow holds the top seed with an entry time of 1:57.74.

Kitching will kick off the prelims at 9:58 p.m. on Thursday, looking to book her ticket to the finals at 9:43 p.m. on Saturday. She’ll compete in the first heat, where she holds the only sub-two minute mark of the eight competitors.

Men’s 100m

Sophomore Ajani Dwyer has been running his best over the past month, making his way to the biggest stage. He finished fifth at the Big Ten Championships with a wind-aided time of 9.93 seconds and has since run under 10 seconds in his last two races.

Dwyer enters with the fourth-best time in the nation, behind Auburn’s Kanyinsola Ajayi and Israel Okon and Middle Tennessee’s Mustapha Bokpin.

The first round will kick off at 9:25 p.m. on Wednesday, with the two best runners from each heat and the next three best times qualifying for the finals at 8:52 p.m. on Friday.

Dwyer will race in the first heat on Wednesday, where he has the top time by 0.04 seconds. His biggest challenger in earning the heat’s best time is Tennessee’s Traunard Folson, who has a season-best 10.04 seconds.

Men’s 1500m

After finishing one place shy of an NCAA Championship berth during the indoor season, Nick Sloff is back with the No. 19 mark in the nation.

The State College native will compete at 8:21 p.m. on Wednesday, looking to be one of the best five runners in his heat or two next-best times to compete in the finals at 8:12 p.m. on Friday.

Sloff will compete in the first heat, where he has the 10th-best time behind Oregon’s Simeon Birnbaum, Washington’s Martin Barco and others.

Women’s shot put

Penn State’s second and only other women’s entry comes from sophomore Sasha Garnett, who burst onto the scene as she tied for the third-best shot put mark in school history.

Garnett enters the NCAA Championship with the No. 18 mark in the country with 16.88 meters. She’ll throw at 10:10 p.m. on Wednesday in a pool of 24 throwers, including Nebraska’s Axelina Johansson — the nation’s top thrower.

Men’s shot put

The Nittany Lions two entries in the men’s shot put, Dylan Lambrecht and Ryan Henry, have been battling it out all season.

Lambrecht took the lead with the No. 5 mark in school history of 19.58 meters, good for fifth in the nation. A few weeks prior, Henry threw 19.48 meters, which is eighth in school history and seeded 11th overall.

The two will throw at 10:10 p.m. on Wednesday, as they look to overtake Oregon’s Ben Smith and Nebraska’s Hencu Lamberts, who’ve each thrown for over 20 meters this season.

Men’s hammer

After four years at the regional qualifying meet, Collin Burkhart booked his ticket to nationals in his final go-around.

Burkhart holds the No. 3 mark in school history and the No. 14 mark in the nation with 67.53 meters, and will throw at 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday against six athletes who’ve thrown over 70 meters this season.

title: ‘He was like family’ | Those close to Penn State's Dr. Wayne Sebastianelli remember his legacy
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Against Ohio State in 2000, Adam Taliaferro laid motionless on the field after tackling Jerry Westbrooks. As he regained consciousness, he locked eyes with Dr. Wayne Sebastianelli.

In the scariest moment of Taliaferro’s life and a pivotal one in Sebastianelli’s, Penn State’s director of athletic medicine remained calm despite the gravity of the situation. He administered a steroid to Taliaferro in the moments after the cornerback’s injury, which became a key part in his recovery from paralysis just a few months later.

“I remember opening up my eyes on the field, and he was the first eyes that I looked into when I was laying on the field panicking because I couldn’t move,” Taliaferro told The Daily Collegian. “The first thing he told me was, ‘Adam, you’re going to be okay.’”

Throughout Sebastianelli’s career, he cared for thousands of athletes like Taliaferro, as well as hundreds of thousands of people across Central Pennsylvania. As one of the nation’s most respected orthopedic surgeons, Sebastianelli continued seeing patients until his death on May 4.

After serving as a clinical instructor of surgery at Penn State's Milton Hershey Medical Center, Sebastianelli’s career took a new direction in 1992 when he was named the director of athletic medicine at Penn State. There, he spent more than three decades working across Athletics, including two long stints with the football team.

In addition to his roles within athletics, Sebastianelli also served as the associate chief medical officer for Penn State Health in Centre County and as associate dean for clinical affairs at the University Park Regional Campus. He was also chief of staff at Mount Nittany Medical Center and Mount Nittany Surgical Center.

“I don’t think that someone can devote that much time, effort, blood and sweat into taking care of the student athletes than he did,” Dr. Gregory Billy, a 15-year team physician for Penn State Athletics and primary care physician for Penn State football, told the Collegian. “The longevity and commitment was intense.”

Sebastianelli not only helped advance athletic medicine for Penn State, but he also helped push the field forward across the industry. When he started with the Nittany Lions, a visiting orthopedic doctor, often from Hershey, would travel twice a week to check on athletes, but Sebastianelli pushed for a permanent orthopedics department.

Penn State Athletics’ orthopedics department now has five surgeons and five primary care doctors, showcasing Sebastianelli’s commitment to advance medicine for the Nittany Lions.

“The way that he structured the athletic department, he would have 100% autonomy so the coaches couldn’t influence his decisions,” Jay Paterno, former Penn State assistant coach and current Board of Trustees member, told the Collegian. “That structuring of the athletic department and the medical services was way ahead of its time.”

Sebastianelli’s knowledge and background went far beyond orthopedic surgery. He also played a significant role in advancing concussion evaluation and treatment.

Paterno said Penn State was “doing things in concussion protocol before any of the major sports leagues were doing it,” a critical, highly beneficial advancement for athletes.

“He was part of a very forward-thinking, visionary way of looking at things, and Penn State benefited for many years,” Paterno said.

But Sebastianelli will also be remembered for the relationships he developed with players, coaches and administrators. Taliaferro said Sebastianelli flew down to Philadelphia weekly to see him during his recovery and “would always leave with a kiss on my forehead and saying he loves me.”

Sebastianelli also had a big impact on Michael Mauti’s time at Penn State, as he performed the first major orthopedic surgeries of Mauti’s career when he repaired both of the linebacker’s torn ACLs.

“Establishing a genuine relationship with guys was a big thing,” Mauti told the Collegian. “He always had this warm aura around him that showed how much he cared about being the best (doctor) he could be for each individual.”

Despite the high-stakes nature of his job, Sebastianelli built these relationships with the athletes through shared interests, bringing a human touch to his interactions.

Billy said they would always have engaging conversations with student athletes, whether it was about song preferences or NBA players.

“We’d have players that would come back after 30 years, and he’d know every single player and they’d shake his hand or hug him,” Billy said.

Sebastianelli also provided care to various members of the coaching staff, including Joe Paterno.

When Joe Paterno needed a hip replacement in 2008 after an injury from practicing onside kicks, Sebastianelli provided the surgery and was with him throughout the recovery process.

“Joe trusted him,” Paterno said. “There were few doctors my dad was excited about. He hates doctors, but he had great respect for Wayne, loved him, and Wayne was like family to us.”

The two’s relationship extended far beyond the football field or the operating room. Over their two decades together, Sebastianelli became a close friend to the Paterno family. That trust continued until Joe’s death in 2012, with Sebastianelli in the room with his family when Joe died.

While that family feeling was felt between Sebastianelli and the Paterno family, a similar level of care was felt by all the players Sebastianelli treated.

“He was one of the reasons I got to continue living my dream,” Mauti said. “It’s hard, you just don’t repay something like that. He wasn’t really like a surgeon to the lettermen, he was like family.”

Additionally, Sebastianelli served as a mentor to younger doctors in the field. He helped train many fellows at Penn State and helped younger doctors in the program.

For those physicians, Sebastianelli helped shape how they approached patient care. His influence spanned everywhere in the Penn State community, a fact the lettermen saw firsthand.

“He’s one of the most caring, genuine people you’ll ever meet,” Taliaferro said. “It doesn’t matter if you were a football player or a 90-year-old grandmom in State College, he was going to give you the same level of care and compassion. His craft meant the world to him, and to have shared his gift with so many of us that have been injured along the way, we’re all so blessed.”

In remembering Sebastianelli, those who were treated by him highlighted the lasting impact he had on their lives. Penn State lettermen, former coaches and community members have poured out praise for Sebastianelli in the weeks since his death.

“I’m just so thankful that the world is now really getting to understand the amount of people he truly helped over his wonderful career at Penn State,” Taliaferro said. “He was one of the pillars across all of Athletics and in the community.”

title: Penn State student shot and killed outside of Philadelphia home
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Penn State student was shot and killed outside of his Philadelphia home early Saturday morning, according to 6abc.

22-year-old William “Billy” Schmidt was a fourth-semester student studying digital journalism and media at World Campus, confirmed a Penn State spokesperson.

Schmidt was reportedly shot around 1:30 a.m. Saturday near the 1900 block of Durfor Street. According to ABC News, he was found lying in the road with a gunshot wound by police and transported to the hospital where he was later declared dead at 1:47 a.m.

His father, Bill Schmidt, told 6abc that his son was walking home from a local bar after watching the NBA finals with friends.

"He was a really good person who cared about everybody and never hurt or bothered a soul, never bothered anyone and for him to get shot like that is a travesty," Bill Schmidt said to 6abc.

According to 6abc, nearby surveillance camera footage reportedly showed a dispute over a cellphone. In the footage, a man throws a cellphone and another man runs around a corner with Schmidt chasing after him. That man then turned around and shot Schmidt.

“I'm shocked when they stole his phone that he chased them," Bill Schmidt told 6abc. "Supposedly when he got to the corner, another came out and he shot him.”

Penn State spokesperson Wyatt DuBois said in a statement to the Collegian that “We are heartbroken over the tragic death of William Schmidt, and we share our deepest condolences with his family and friends.”

DuBois also shared the counseling services available to students at this time.

He said Penn State World Campus students can connect with a case manager through the Chaiken Center for Student Success. Commonwealth Campus students can find Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) information for their campus online. University Park students can contact CAPS at 814-863-0395.

The 24-hour Penn State Crisis Line is available to all Penn State students by calling 1-877-229-6400 or texting LIONS to 741741.

The Philadelphia Police Department has not announced any arrests or motives yet, and urged anyone with information to contact the homicide unit at 215-686-3334 or submit an anonymous tip at 215-686-TIPS.

Schmidt’s family members have created a GoFundMe page to cover “essential costs” and “provide some stability as we navigate the days ahead.” It has raised nearly $55,000 of its $70,000 goal as of Monday afternoon.

“Our family is heartbroken by his sudden and tragic passing,” Anna Schmidt, Billy’s sister, said in the page’s description. “Billy was a beloved brother, friend, and member of our community, and his loss has left us all in shock.”
